{{Shrine | Name = {{ill|Sonai Shrine|ja|園相神社}} | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = [[File:Sonai-jinja 01.jpg|280px]] | Location = 723 Shiraki, Tsumura-cho, [[Ise]], [[Mie Prefecture]], Japan | Coordinates = {{coord|34|27|25.1|N|136|39|19.7|E|region:JP|display=inline}} | Map = Japan Mie | Deity = * {{ill|Sonaibihiko-no-mikoto|ja|曽奈比比古命}} * {{ill|Mimae-no-kami|ja|御前神}} | Shrine Rank = [[Shikinaisha]] (minor rank), {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮}} auxiliary shrine (*sessha*) | Established = Unknown | Main Hall = [[Shinmei-zukuri]] | Other Names = Shiraki-san (白木さん) | Annual Festival = [[Kannamesai]] (Harvest Festival) | Rituals = }}
'''{{ill|Sonai Shrine|ja|園相神社}}''' (*Sonai Jinja*) is an auxiliary shrine (*sessha*) of {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮}} (the Inner Shrine of the [[Ise Grand Shrine]]), located in [[Ise]], [[Mie Prefecture]], Japan. It ranks third among the auxiliary shrines of the Inner Shrine.<ref>Ujiyamada City Office (1929): pp. 9–11.</ref>
The shrine enshrines deities who serve as the guardians of the sacred gardens (*御園*, *misono*) of the Grand Shrine.<ref name="gk">Gakken Publishing (2013): p. 65.</ref><ref name="ic">Ise Bunka Publishing (2008): p. 71.</ref>
== 概要 == [[三重県]][[伊勢市]]津村町[[小字|字]]白木723に鎮座する<ref name="tm"/>。[[倭姫命]]が定めた神社であるという<ref name="gk"/>。[[地域]]では鎮座[[地名]]を取って<ref name="tm"/>「白木さん」、「しろきの宮」と呼ばれ、厚い[[信仰]]を集めている<ref name="ic"/>。
まっすぐに伸びた[[スギ|杉]]の木立を抜け、長い[[参道]]を進むと[[社殿]]が現れる<ref name="ic"/>。参道入り口に[[享保]][[甲辰]]（1724年）の銘がある禁殺生石が建つ<ref name="sk1">式内社研究会 編（1990）：221ページ</ref>。[[1990年]]（平成2年）時点の社地の面積は5,609[[平方メートル|m<sup>2</sup>]]であったが、隣接する[[三重県道22号伊勢南島線]]の拡幅工事のため753.9m<sup>2</sup>を三重県に譲渡した<ref name="sk1"/>。社殿は神明造の板葺で、大きさは行9尺（≒2.7m）×妻7尺（≒2.1m）×高さ7.8尺（≒2.4m）である<ref name="sk1"/>。玉垣・玉垣御門・鳥居を備える<ref name="sk1"/>。[[古代]]の社殿は、長さ9尺（≒2.7m）×広さ7.5尺（≒2.3m）×高さ4.6尺（≒1.4m）の社殿を2棟有した<ref name="jg">福山ほか（1975）：150ページ</ref>。[[神体]]は石である<ref name="jg"/>。
== 祭神 == [[祭神]]は、曽奈比比古命（そないひこのみこと）、御前神（みまえのかみ）<ref name="gk"/><ref name="tm"/><ref name="ic"/>。この地域の神であり<ref name="gk"/>、神宮の御園の[[守護神]]である<ref name="ic"/>。曽奈比比古命は大水上命（おおみなかみのみこと）の子であり、園作神（そのつくりのかみ）である<ref name="tm"/>。
[[歯痛]]にご利益があるという信仰がかつて存在し、全快すると萩の箸を12膳奉納する[[お礼参り]]の慣習があった<ref name="ic"/>。
古代には御前神を祀る御前社が存在していたが、[[文永]]3年（[[1266年]]）に造り替えられて以降、社殿の造り替えは行われなくなり、後に御前社は建てられなくなり、御前神は曽奈比比古命を祀る本社と同座することとなった<ref>式内社研究会 編（1990）：219 - 220ページ</ref>。
== 歴史 == [[宮川 (三重県)|宮川]]に沿って下ってきた倭姫命は、ここで園作神に[[天照大神]]への御園を奉られたため、この神社を定めたという<ref name="ic"/><ref name="sk0">式内社研究会 編（1990）：220ページ</ref>。[[天津神・国津神|国津神]]を祭神とする神社は[[正税]]によって建て替えが行われたが、園相神社を含む格式の高い6社は内宮の[[遷御]]が完了すると造神宮使によって建て替えられた<ref>金子（1983）：60 - 61ページ</ref>。『文永三年内宮遷宮記』に遷御が行われた記録が残されている<ref name="sk9">式内社研究会 編（1990）：219ページ</ref>。しかし[[応仁]]（[[1467年]] - [[1468年]]）の頃から造り替えが遅れ始め、やがて造り替えが行われないどころか社地すら不明となってしまった<ref name="sk9"/>。
[[寛文]]3年（[[1663年]]）、大宮司の[[河辺精長|河邊精長]]（大中臣精長）は旧社地の探索を行い、『皇大神宮儀式帳』の記述「在沼木郷積良」などを参照し、度会郡積良村（現・[[玉城町]]積良）に[[9月22日 (旧暦)|9月22日]]（[[グレゴリオ暦]]：1663年[[10月22日]]）に社殿を造営、[[12月9日 (旧暦)|12月9日]]（グレゴリオ暦：[[1664年]][[1月7日]]）に神遷を行って再興した<ref name="sk9"/>。ただし再興地は疑義が出され、[[元禄]]4年[[12月23日 (旧暦)|12月23日]]（グレゴリオ暦：[[1692年]][[2月9日]]）に大中臣長春は津村を旧社地であると定め、元禄7年[[3月26日 (旧暦)|3月26日]]（グレゴリオ暦：[[1694年]][[4月20日]]）に神遷を行って現社地に移転した<ref name="sk9"/>。
[[1958年]]（昭和33年）3月に造り替えが行われ、[[1981年]]（昭和56年）4月に大修繕が施された<ref name="sk1"/>。
== 祭祀 == 三節祭（[[月次祭]]・[[神嘗祭]]）、[[祈年祭]]、[[新嘗祭]]、臨時奉幣祭の際には[[神職]]らが訪れ、社頭で祭祀を執り行う<ref name="sk0"/>。それ以外の[[歳旦祭]]などは内宮の五丈殿で遥祀を行う<ref name="sk0"/>。
なお日常の[[清掃]]等を行う役職として「[[祝部]]」が置かれており、神宮司庁から任命を受けた祝部が神社の奉護に当たる<ref name="sk0"/>。
== 植物相 == [[File:Sonai-jinja 02.jpg|thumb|三重県道22号伊勢南島線と園相神社の社叢]] [[伊勢湾台風]]を耐えた[[ヒノキ]]・スギ・[[クロマツ]]の大木を主体とし、[[サカキ]]・[[シイ]]・[[クロバイ]]・[[ヒサカキ]]などの[[常緑広葉樹]]、[[落葉広葉樹]]が混成した[[社叢]]が形成されている<ref name="sk0"/>。
== 周辺 == 神社の入り口は三重県道22号伊勢南島線に面している<ref name="gk"/><ref>伊勢文化舎 編（2008）：70ページ</ref>。車道沿いにありながら、境内は静寂に満ちている<ref name="ic"/>。[[山田 (伊勢市)|伊勢市街]]方面から訪れると、[[伊勢自動車道]]の高架をくぐったところにある<ref name="ic"/>。 * [[東海旅客鉄道|JR]][[参宮線]]・[[近畿日本鉄道|近鉄]][[近鉄山田線|山田線]][[伊勢市駅]]より[[三重交通]][[路線バス|バス]]乗車、白木[[バス停留所|バス停]]下車すぐ<ref name="tm"/>（バス乗車距離約7.5[[キロメートル|km]]<ref name="sk9"/>、19分<ref name="tm"/>）。 * 伊勢自動車道[[玉城インターチェンジ|玉城IC]]より[[自動車]]で約11分（約7.5km）、[[伊勢西インターチェンジ|伊勢西IC]]より自動車で約27分（約7.3km）<!--Yahoo!地図によるルート検索結果より-->。
== 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist|2}}
== 参考文献 == * [[伊勢文化舎]] 編『お伊勢さん125社めぐり』別冊『伊勢人』、伊勢文化舎、平成20年12月23日、151p. ISBN 978-4-900759-37-4 * 宇治山田市役所 編『宇治山田市史 上巻』宇治山田市役所、昭和4年1月20日、862p. * [[学研パブリッシング]]『伊勢神宮に行こう』Gakken Mook神社紀行セレクションvol.1、[[薗田稔]]監修、学研マーケティング、2013年7月4日、82p. ISBN 978-4-05-610047-1 * 金子延夫『玉城町史 第1巻―南伊勢の歴史と伝承―』三重県郷土資料叢書第61集、三重県郷土史料刊行会、昭和58年8月10日、276p. * 式内社研究会 編『式内社調査報告 第六巻 東海道1』[[皇學館大学]]出版部、平成2年2月28日、690p. ISBN 4-87644-080-8 * [[福山敏男]]・[[稲垣栄三|稲垣榮三]]・村瀬美樹・胡麻鶴醇之『神宮―第六十回神宮式年遷宮―』[[小学館]]、昭和50年4月20日、246p. * 『日本人なら知っておきたい 伊勢神宮と125の社』タツミムック、[[辰巳出版]]、2013年11月1日、127p. ISBN 978-4-7778-1197-7
== 関連項目 == * {{ill|List of 125 Shrines of Ise|ja|神宮125社の一覧}} * [[宮本村 (三重県)]]
== 外部リンク == {{ external media | align = | width = 230px | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image1 = [http://www.sengukan.jp/wp-content/themes/sengukan/images/shrine/area05/area05_01.jpg 園相神社] }} * [http://www.jingukaikan.jp/125mairi/m06/0608.html 園相神社（そないじんじゃ）] - 一般財団法人伊勢神宮崇敬会

{{Shrine | Name = {{ill|Sonai Shrine|ja|園相神社}} | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = [[File:Sonai-jinja 01.jpg|280px]] | location = 723 Shiraki, Tsumura-cho, [[Ise]], [[Mie Prefecture]], Japan | Coordinates = {{coord|34|27|25.1|N|136|39|19.7|E|region:JP|display=inline}} | Map = Japan Mie | deity = * {{ill|Sonaibihiko-no-mikoto|ja|曽奈比比古命}} * {{ill|Mimae-no-kami|ja|御前神}} | type = [[Shikinaisha]] (minor rank), {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮}} auxiliary shrine (*sessha*) | Established = Unknown | Main Hall = [[Shinmei-zukuri]] | Other Names = Shiraki-san (白木さん) | festival = [[Kannamesai]] (Harvest Festival) | Rituals = }}
'''{{ill|Sonai Shrine|ja|園相神社}}''' (*Sonai Jinja*) is an auxiliary shrine (*sessha*) of {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮}} (the Inner Shrine of the [[Ise Grand Shrine]]), located in [[Ise]], [[Mie Prefecture]], Japan. It ranks third among the auxiliary shrines of the Inner Shrine.<ref>Ujiyamada City Office (1929): pp. 9–11.</ref>
The shrine enshrines deities who serve as the guardians of the sacred gardens (*御園*, *misono*) of the Grand Shrine.<ref name="gk">Gakken Publishing (2013): p. 65.</ref><ref name="ic">Ise Bunka Publishing (2008): p. 71.</ref>

[[寛文]]3年（[[1663年]]）、大宮司の[[河辺精長|河邊精長]]（大中臣精長）は旧社地の探索を行い、『皇大神宮儀式帳』の記述「在沼木郷積良」などを参照し、度会郡積良村（現・[[玉城町]]積良）に[[9月22日 (旧暦)|9月22日]]（[[グレゴリオ暦]]：1663年[[10月22日]]）に社殿を造営、[[12月9日 (旧暦)|12月9日]]（グレゴリオ暦：[[1664年]][[1月7日]]）に神遷を行って再興した<ref name="sk9"/>。ただし再興地は疑義が出され、[[元禄]]4年[[12月23日 (旧暦)|12月23日]]（グレゴリオ暦：[[1692年]][[2月9日]]）に大中臣長春は津村を旧社地であると定め、元禄7年[[3月26日 (旧暦)|3月26日]]（グレゴリオ暦：[[1694年]][[4月20日]]）に神遷を行って現社地に移転した<ref name="sk9"/>。

{{Shrine | Name = {{ill|Sonai Shrine|ja|園相神社}} | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = [[File:Sonai-jinja 01.jpg|280px]] | location = 723 Shiraki, Tsumura-cho, [[Ise]], [[Mie Prefecture]], Japan | Coordinates = {{coord|34|27|25.1|N|136|39|19.7|E|region:JP|display=inline}} | Map = Japan Mie | deity = * {{ill|Sonaibihiko-no-mikoto|ja|曽奈比比古命}} * {{ill|Mimae-no-kami|ja|御前神}} | type = [[Shikinaisha]] (minor rank), {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮}} auxiliary shrine (*sessha*) | Established = Unknown | Main Hall = [[Shinmei-zukuri]] | Other Names = Shiraki-san (白木さん) | festival = [[Kannamesai]] (Harvest Festival) | Rituals = }}
'''{{ill|Sonai Shrine|ja|園相神社}}''' (*Sonai Jinja*) is an auxiliary shrine (*sessha*) of {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮}} (the Inner Shrine of the [[Ise Grand Shrine]]), located in [[Ise]], [[Mie Prefecture]], Japan. It ranks third among the auxiliary shrines of the Inner Shrine.<ref>Ujiyamada City Office (1929): pp. 9–11.</ref>
The shrine enshrines deities who serve as the guardians of the sacred gardens (*御園*, *misono*) of the Grand Shrine.<ref name="gk">Gakken Publishing (2013): p. 65.</ref><ref name="ic">Ise Bunka Publishing (2008): p. 71.</ref>
== Overview ==
Located at 723 Shiraki, Tsumura-chō, {{ill|Ise, Mie|ja|伊勢市}}, {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県}}.<ref name="tm"/> This shrine is said to have been established by {{ill|Yamatohime-no-mikoto|ja|倭姫命}}.<ref name="gk"/> Locally, it is affectionately referred to as "Shiraki-san" or "Shiroki-no-miya," and has garnered deep devotion.<ref name="ic"/>
Walking through a straight path lined with tall {{ill|cedar trees|ja|杉}}, one reaches the shrine buildings.<ref name="ic"/> At the entrance of the path stands a stone inscribed in {{ill|Kyōhō|ja|享保}} (1724), prohibiting the killing of living beings.<ref name="sk1">Shikinaisha Research Association (1990): p. 221</ref> As of 1990 (Heisei 2), the shrine grounds covered 5,609 m², but 753.9 m² were transferred to Mie Prefecture for the expansion of {{ill|Mie Prefectural Route 22 Ise-Nantō Line|ja|三重県道22号伊勢南島線}}.<ref name="sk1"/> The shrine buildings are constructed in the {{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|ja|神明造}} style with board roofing, measuring approximately 9 shaku (≈2.7 m) in length, 7 shaku (≈2.1 m) in width, and 7.8 shaku (≈2.4 m) in height.<ref name="sk1"/> They are equipped with a sacred fence ({{ill|tamagaki|ja|玉垣}}), a tamagaki gate, and a {{ill|torii|ja|鳥居}}.<ref name="sk1"/>
In ancient times, there were two shrine buildings, each measuring 9 shaku (≈2.7 m) in length, 7.5 shaku (≈2.3 m) in width, and 4.6 shaku (≈1.4 m) in height.<ref name="jg">Fukuyama et al. (1975): p. 150</ref> The sacred object is a stone.<ref name="jg"/>
== Enshrined Deities ==
The enshrined deities are Sonaibihiko-no-Mikoto (曽奈比比古命) and Gozen-no-Kami (御前神).<ref name="gk"/><ref name="tm"/><ref name="ic"/> They are local deities of this region<ref name="gk"/> and protectors of the sacred gardens of Ise Grand Shrine.<ref name="ic"/> Sonaibihiko-no-Mikoto is the child of Ōminakami-no-Mikoto (大水上命) and is known as the deity of garden creation.<ref name="tm"/>
There was once a belief that the shrine could cure toothaches, and it was customary to offer 12 pairs of chopsticks made from bush clover as a token of gratitude upon recovery.<ref name="ic"/>
In ancient times, there was an independent shrine dedicated to Gozen-no-Kami, but after its reconstruction in {{ill|Bunei|ja|文永}} 3 (1266), no further reconstructions took place. Eventually, the shrine was not rebuilt, and Gozen-no-Kami came to be enshrined together with Sonaibihiko-no-Mikoto in the main shrine.<ref>Shikinaisha Research Association (1990): pp. 219–220</ref>
== History ==
It is said that when {{ill|Yamatohime-no-mikoto|ja|倭姫命}} traveled along the {{ill|Miyagawa River (Mie)|ja|宮川}}, she dedicated sacred gardens to {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神}} at this location, thus establishing the shrine.<ref name="ic"/><ref name="sk0">Shikinaisha Research Association (1990): p. 220</ref> Shrines enshrining {{ill|kunitsukami|ja|国津神}} were traditionally rebuilt using public funds, but six highly ranked shrines, including this one, were rebuilt by the shrine’s construction officials after the completion of the Inner Shrine’s transfer ceremony.<ref>Kaneko (1983): pp. 60–61</ref> Records of such a ceremony can be found in the ''Bunei 3 Naikū Sengu-ki''.<ref name="sk9">Shikinaisha Research Association (1990): p. 219</ref>
However, from around the {{ill|Ōnin era|ja|応仁}} (1467–1468), reconstruction efforts began to delay, and eventually, not only did reconstruction cease, but the location of the shrine grounds also became unknown.<ref name="sk9"/>
In {{ill|Kanbun|ja|寛文}} 3 (1663), the chief priest {{ill|Kawabe Masanaga|ja|河邊精長}} (Ōnakatomi Masanaga) conducted a search for the old shrine grounds. Referring to descriptions in the ''Kōtai Jingū Gishiki-chō'' such as "located in Numaki-go Sekiryo," the shrine was reconstructed in Sekiryo Village (present-day {{ill|Tamaki, Mie|ja|玉城町}} Sekiryo) on {{ill|September 22|ja|9月22日}} (Gregorian calendar: October 22, 1663), and the deity was transferred on {{ill|December 9|ja|12月9日}} (Gregorian calendar: January 7, 1664).<ref name="sk9"/>
However, doubts arose regarding this location, and on {{ill|December 23|ja|12月23日}} of {{ill|Genroku|ja|元禄}} 4 (Gregorian calendar: February 9, 1692), Ōnakatomi Nagaharu determined that Tsumura was the original site. The shrine was relocated to its current location on {{ill|March 26|ja|3月26日}} of Genroku 7 (Gregorian calendar: April 20, 1694).<ref name="sk9"/>
The shrine was rebuilt in March {{ill|1958|ja|1958年}} (Shōwa 33), and major renovations were carried out in April {{ill|1981|ja|1981年}} (Shōwa 56).<ref name="sk1"/>
== Rituals ==
During major festivals such as {{ill|Tsukinami-sai|ja|月次祭}}, {{ill|Kannamesai|ja|神嘗祭}}, {{ill|Kinensai|ja|祈年祭}}, {{ill|Niinamesai|ja|新嘗祭}}, and temporary offerings, priests visit the shrine to conduct rituals at the shrine front.<ref name="sk0"/> Other ceremonies, such as the {{ill|Saitan-sai|ja|歳旦祭}}, are conducted remotely from the Gojōden of the Inner Shrine.<ref name="sk0"/>
Additionally, the role of {{ill|hafuri (Shinto)|ja|祝部}} is designated to carry out daily maintenance and shrine duties. The hafuri are appointed by the Jingū Administration Office.<ref name="sk0"/>
== Flora ==
[[File:Sonai-jinja 02.jpg|thumb|Sacred forest of Sonai Shrine and Mie Prefectural Route 22 Ise-Nantō Line]] The shrine forest is composed of large trees that survived the {{ill|Isewan Typhoon|ja|伊勢湾台風}}, including {{ill|Japanese cypress|ja|ヒノキ}}, {{ill|cedar|ja|スギ}}, and {{ill|Japanese black pine|ja|クロマツ}}. It also contains a mixture of evergreen broadleaf trees such as {{ill|sakaki|ja|サカキ}}, {{ill|Japanese chinquapin|ja|シイ}}, {{ill|black oak|ja|クロバイ}}, and {{ill|Japanese cleyera|ja|ヒサカキ}}, along with deciduous broadleaf trees.<ref name="sk0"/>
== Surroundings ==
The shrine entrance faces {{ill|Mie Prefectural Route 22 Ise-Nantō Line|ja|三重県道22号伊勢南島線}}.<ref name="gk"/><ref>Ise Bunka-sha (2008): p. 70</ref> Despite its location along the roadway, the precincts remain peaceful and serene.<ref name="ic"/> Visitors coming from {{ill|Yamada (Ise)|ja|山田 (伊勢市)}} pass under the overpass of the {{ill|Ise Expressway|ja|伊勢自動車道}} to reach the shrine.<ref name="ic"/>
* From {{ill|Ise-shi Station|ja|伊勢市駅}} on the {{ill|Sangū Line|ja|参宮線}} operated by {{ill|JR Central|ja|東海旅客鉄道}} or the {{ill|Kintetsu Yamada Line|ja|近鉄山田線}} operated by {{ill|Kintetsu|ja|近畿日本鉄道}}, take a {{ill|Mie Kotsu|ja|三重交通}} bus and get off at the ''Shiraki'' bus stop. The shrine is located immediately nearby.<ref name="tm"/> (Approx. 7.5 km bus distance,<ref name="sk9"/> about 19 minutes.<ref name="tm"/>)
* By car, it takes about 11 minutes (approx. 7.5 km) from {{ill|Tamaki IC|ja|玉城インターチェンジ}} on the Ise Expressway, or about 27 minutes (approx. 7.3 km) from {{ill|Ise-nishi IC|ja|伊勢西インターチェンジ}}.
[[Category:Shinto shrines in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:Auxiliary shrines of Ise Grand Shrine]] [[Category:Sacred forests of Japan]] [[ja:園相神社]]

ISBN 978-4-900759-37-4 * 宇治山田市役所 編『宇治山田市史 上巻』宇治山田市役所、昭和4年1月20日、862p. * [[学研パブリッシング]]『伊勢神宮に行こう』Gakken Mook神社紀行セレクションvol.1、[[薗田稔]]監修、学研マーケティング、2013年7月4日、82p. ISBN 978-4-05-610047-1 * 金子延夫『玉城町史 第1巻―南伊勢の歴史と伝承―』三重県郷土資料叢書第61集、三重県郷土史料刊行会、昭和58年8月10日、276p. * 式内社研究会 編『式内社調査報告 第六巻 東海道1』[[皇學館大学]]出版部、平成2年2月28日、690p. ISBN 4-87644-080-8 * [[福山敏男]]・[[稲垣栄三|稲垣榮三]]・村瀬美樹・胡麻鶴醇之『神宮―第六十回神宮式年遷宮―』[[小学館]]、昭和50年4月20日、246p. * 『日本人なら知っておきたい 伊勢神宮と125の社』タツミムック、[[辰巳出版]]、2013年11月1日、127p. ISBN 978-4-7778-1197-7

{{Shrine | Name = {{ill|Sonai Shrine|ja|園相神社}} | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = [[File:Sonai-jinja 01.jpg|280px]] | location = 723 Shiraki, Tsumura-cho, [[Ise]], [[Mie Prefecture]], Japan | Coordinates = {{coord|34|27|25.1|N|136|39|19.7|E|region:JP|display=inline}} | Map = Japan Mie | deity = * {{ill|Sonaibihiko-no-mikoto|ja|曽奈比比古命}} * {{ill|Mimae-no-kami|ja|御前神}} | type = [[Shikinaisha]] (minor rank), {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮}} auxiliary shrine (*sessha*) | Established = Unknown | Main Hall = [[Shinmei-zukuri]] | Other Names = Shiraki-san (白木さん) | festival = [[Kannamesai]] (Harvest Festival) | Rituals = }}
'''{{ill|Sonai Shrine|ja|園相神社}}''' (*Sonai Jinja*) is an auxiliary shrine (*sessha*) of {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮}} (the Inner Shrine of the [[Ise Grand Shrine]]), located in [[Ise]], [[Mie Prefecture]], Japan. It ranks third among the auxiliary shrines of the Inner Shrine.<ref>Ujiyamada City Office (1929): pp. 9–11.</ref>
The shrine enshrines deities who serve as the guardians of the sacred gardens (*御園*, *misono*) of the Grand Shrine.<ref name="gk">Gakken Publishing (2013): p. 65.</ref><ref name="ic">Ise Bunka Publishing (2008): p. 71.</ref>
== Overview ==
Located at 723 Shiraki, Tsumura-chō, {{ill|Ise, Mie|ja|伊勢市}}, {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県}}.<ref name="tm"/> This shrine is said to have been established by {{ill|Yamatohime-no-mikoto|ja|倭姫命}}.<ref name="gk"/> Locally, it is affectionately referred to as "Shiraki-san" or "Shiroki-no-miya," and has garnered deep devotion.<ref name="ic"/>
Walking through a straight path lined with tall {{ill|cedar trees|ja|杉}}, one reaches the shrine buildings.<ref name="ic"/> At the entrance of the path stands a stone inscribed in {{ill|Kyōhō|ja|享保}} (1724), prohibiting the killing of living beings.<ref name="sk1">Shikinaisha Research Association (1990): p. 221</ref> As of 1990 (Heisei 2), the shrine grounds covered 5,609 m², but 753.9 m² were transferred to Mie Prefecture for the expansion of {{ill|Mie Prefectural Route 22 Ise-Nantō Line|ja|三重県道22号伊勢南島線}}.<ref name="sk1"/> The shrine buildings are constructed in the {{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|ja|神明造}} style with board roofing, measuring approximately 9 shaku (≈2.7 m) in length, 7 shaku (≈2.1 m) in width, and 7.8 shaku (≈2.4 m) in height.<ref name="sk1"/> They are equipped with a sacred fence ({{ill|tamagaki|ja|玉垣}}), a tamagaki gate, and a {{ill|torii|ja|鳥居}}.<ref name="sk1"/>
In ancient times, there were two shrine buildings, each measuring 9 shaku (≈2.7 m) in length, 7.5 shaku (≈2.3 m) in width, and 4.6 shaku (≈1.4 m) in height.<ref name="jg">Fukuyama et al. (1975): p. 150</ref> The sacred object is a stone.<ref name="jg"/>
== Enshrined Deities ==
The enshrined deities are Sonaibihiko-no-Mikoto (曽奈比比古命) and Gozen-no-Kami (御前神).<ref name="gk"/><ref name="tm"/><ref name="ic"/> They are local deities of this region<ref name="gk"/> and protectors of the sacred gardens of Ise Grand Shrine.<ref name="ic"/> Sonaibihiko-no-Mikoto is the child of Ōminakami-no-Mikoto (大水上命) and is known as the deity of garden creation.<ref name="tm"/>
There was once a belief that the shrine could cure toothaches, and it was customary to offer 12 pairs of chopsticks made from bush clover as a token of gratitude upon recovery.<ref name="ic"/>
In ancient times, there was an independent shrine dedicated to Gozen-no-Kami, but after its reconstruction in {{ill|Bunei|ja|文永}} 3 (1266), no further reconstructions took place. Eventually, the shrine was not rebuilt, and Gozen-no-Kami came to be enshrined together with Sonaibihiko-no-Mikoto in the main shrine.<ref>Shikinaisha Research Association (1990): pp. 219–220</ref>
== History ==
It is said that when {{ill|Yamatohime-no-mikoto|ja|倭姫命}} traveled along the {{ill|Miyagawa River (Mie)|ja|宮川}}, she dedicated sacred gardens to {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神}} at this location, thus establishing the shrine.<ref name="ic"/><ref name="sk0">Shikinaisha Research Association (1990): p. 220</ref> Shrines enshrining {{ill|kunitsukami|ja|国津神}} were traditionally rebuilt using public funds, but six highly ranked shrines, including this one, were rebuilt by the shrine’s construction officials after the completion of the Inner Shrine’s transfer ceremony.<ref>Kaneko (1983): pp. 60–61</ref> Records of such a ceremony can be found in the ''Bunei 3 Naikū Sengu-ki''.<ref name="sk9">Shikinaisha Research Association (1990): p. 219</ref>
However, from around the {{ill|Ōnin era|ja|応仁}} (1467–1468), reconstruction efforts began to delay, and eventually, not only did reconstruction cease, but the location of the shrine grounds also became unknown.<ref name="sk9"/>
In {{ill|Kanbun|ja|寛文}} 3 (1663), the chief priest {{ill|Kawabe Masanaga|ja|河邊精長}} (Ōnakatomi Masanaga) conducted a search for the old shrine grounds. Referring to descriptions in the ''Kōtai Jingū Gishiki-chō'' such as "located in Numaki-go Sekiryo," the shrine was reconstructed in Sekiryo Village (present-day {{ill|Tamaki, Mie|ja|玉城町}} Sekiryo) on {{ill|September 22|ja|9月22日}} (Gregorian calendar: October 22, 1663), and the deity was transferred on {{ill|December 9|ja|12月9日}} (Gregorian calendar: January 7, 1664).<ref name="sk9"/>
However, doubts arose regarding this location, and on {{ill|December 23|ja|12月23日}} of {{ill|Genroku|ja|元禄}} 4 (Gregorian calendar: February 9, 1692), Ōnakatomi Nagaharu determined that Tsumura was the original site. The shrine was relocated to its current location on {{ill|March 26|ja|3月26日}} of Genroku 7 (Gregorian calendar: April 20, 1694).<ref name="sk9"/>
The shrine was rebuilt in March {{ill|1958|ja|1958年}} (Shōwa 33), and major renovations were carried out in April {{ill|1981|ja|1981年}} (Shōwa 56).<ref name="sk1"/>
== Rituals ==
During major festivals such as {{ill|Tsukinami-sai|ja|月次祭}}, {{ill|Kannamesai|ja|神嘗祭}}, {{ill|Kinensai|ja|祈年祭}}, {{ill|Niinamesai|ja|新嘗祭}}, and temporary offerings, priests visit the shrine to conduct rituals at the shrine front.<ref name="sk0"/> Other ceremonies, such as the {{ill|Saitan-sai|ja|歳旦祭}}, are conducted remotely from the Gojōden of the Inner Shrine.<ref name="sk0"/>
Additionally, the role of {{ill|hafuri (Shinto)|ja|祝部}} is designated to carry out daily maintenance and shrine duties. The hafuri are appointed by the Jingū Administration Office.<ref name="sk0"/>
== Flora ==
[[File:Sonai-jinja 02.jpg|thumb|Sacred forest of Sonai Shrine and Mie Prefectural Route 22 Ise-Nantō Line]] The shrine forest is composed of large trees that survived the {{ill|Isewan Typhoon|ja|伊勢湾台風}}, including {{ill|Japanese cypress|ja|ヒノキ}}, {{ill|cedar|ja|スギ}}, and {{ill|Japanese black pine|ja|クロマツ}}. It also contains a mixture of evergreen broadleaf trees such as {{ill|sakaki|ja|サカキ}}, {{ill|Japanese chinquapin|ja|シイ}}, {{ill|black oak|ja|クロバイ}}, and {{ill|Japanese cleyera|ja|ヒサカキ}}, along with deciduous broadleaf trees.<ref name="sk0"/>
== Surroundings ==
The shrine entrance faces {{ill|Mie Prefectural Route 22 Ise-Nantō Line|ja|三重県道22号伊勢南島線}}.<ref name="gk"/><ref>Ise Bunka-sha (2008): p. 70</ref> Despite its location along the roadway, the precincts remain peaceful and serene.<ref name="ic"/> Visitors coming from {{ill|Yamada (Ise)|ja|山田 (伊勢市)}} pass under the overpass of the {{ill|Ise Expressway|ja|伊勢自動車道}} to reach the shrine.<ref name="ic"/>
* From {{ill|Ise-shi Station|ja|伊勢市駅}} on the {{ill|Sangū Line|ja|参宮線}} operated by {{ill|JR Central|ja|東海旅客鉄道}} or the {{ill|Kintetsu Yamada Line|ja|近鉄山田線}} operated by {{ill|Kintetsu|ja|近畿日本鉄道}}, take a {{ill|Mie Kotsu|ja|三重交通}} bus and get off at the ''Shiraki'' bus stop. The shrine is located immediately nearby.<ref name="tm"/> (Approx. 7.5 km bus distance,<ref name="sk9"/> about 19 minutes.<ref name="tm"/>)
* By car, it takes about 11 minutes (approx. 7.5 km) from {{ill|Tamaki IC|ja|玉城インターチェンジ}} on the Ise Expressway, or about 27 minutes (approx. 7.3 km) from {{ill|Ise-nishi IC|ja|伊勢西インターチェンジ}}.
[[Category:Shinto shrines in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:Auxiliary shrines of Ise Grand Shrine]] [[Category:Sacred forests of Japan]] [[ja:園相神社]]

ISBN 978-4-900759-37-4 * 宇治山田市役所 編『宇治山田市史 上巻』宇治山田市役所、昭和4年1月20日、862p. * [[学研パブリッシング]]『伊勢神宮に行こう』Gakken Mook神社紀行セレクションvol.1、[[薗田稔]]監修、学研マーケティング、2013年7月4日、82p. ISBN 978-4-05-610047-1 * 金子延夫『玉城町史 第1巻―南伊勢の歴史と伝承―』三重県郷土資料叢書第61集、三重県郷土史料刊行会、昭和58年8月10日、276p. * 式内社研究会 編『式内社調査報告 第六巻 東海道1』[[皇學館大学]]出版部、平成2年2月28日、690p. ISBN 4-87644-080-8 * [[福山敏男]]・[[稲垣栄三|稲垣榮三]]・村瀬美樹・胡麻鶴醇之『神宮―第六十回神宮式年遷宮―』[[小学館]]、昭和50年4月20日、246p. * 『日本人なら知っておきたい 伊勢神宮と125の社』タツミムック、[[辰巳出版]]、2013年11月1日、127p. ISBN 978-4-7778-1197-7

'''{{ill|Sonai Shrine|ja|園相神社}}''' (*Sonai Jinja*) is an auxiliary shrine (*sessha*) of {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮}} (the Inner Shrine of the [[Ise Grand Shrine]]), located in [[Ise]], [[Mie Prefecture]], Japan. It ranks third among the auxiliary shrines of the Inner Shrine.<ref>Ujiyamada City Office (1929): pp. 9–11.</ref>

Located at 723 Shiraki, Tsumura-chō, {{ill|Ise, Mie|ja|伊勢市}}, {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県}}.<ref name="tm"/> This shrine is said to have been established by {{ill|Yamatohime-no-mikoto|ja|倭姫命}}.<ref name="gk"/> Locally, it is affectionately referred to as "Shiraki-san" or "Shiroki-no-miya," and has garnered deep devotion.<ref name="ic"/>

The enshrined deities are Sonaibihiko-no-Mikoto (曽奈比比古命) and Gozen-no-Kami (御前神).<ref name="gk"/><ref name="tm"/><ref name="ic"/> They are local deities of this region<ref name="gk"/> and protectors of the sacred gardens of Ise Grand Shrine.<ref name="ic"/> Sonaibihiko-no-Mikoto is the child of Ōminakami-no-Mikoto (大水上命) and is known as the deity of garden creation.<ref name="tm"/>

Eventually, the shrine was not rebuilt, and Gozen-no-Kami came to be enshrined together with Sonaibihiko-no-Mikoto in the main shrine.<ref>Shikinaisha Research Association (1990): pp. 219–220</ref>
== History ==
It is said that when {{ill|Yamatohime-no-mikoto|ja|倭姫命}} traveled along the {{ill|Miyagawa River (Mie)|ja|宮川}}, she dedicated sacred gardens to {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神}} at this location, thus establishing the shrine.<ref name="ic"/><ref name="sk0">Shikinaisha Research Association (1990): p. 220</ref> Shrines enshrining {{ill|kunitsukami|ja|国津神}} were traditionally rebuilt using public funds, but six highly ranked shrines, including this one, were rebuilt by the shrine’s construction officials after the completion of the Inner Shrine’s transfer ceremony.<ref>Kaneko (1983): pp. 60–61</ref> Records of such a ceremony can be found in the ''Bunei 3 Naikū Sengu-ki''.<ref name="sk9">Shikinaisha Research Association (1990): p. 219</ref>
However, from around the {{ill|Ōnin era|ja|応仁}} (1467–1468), reconstruction efforts began to delay, and eventually, not only did reconstruction cease, but the location of the shrine grounds also became unknown.<ref name="sk9"/>
In {{ill|Kanbun|ja|寛文}} 3 (1663), the chief priest {{ill|Kawabe Masanaga|ja|河邊精長}} (Ōnakatomi Masanaga) conducted a search for the old shrine grounds. Referring to descriptions in the ''Kōtai Jingū Gishiki-chō'' such as "located in Numaki-go Sekiryo," the shrine was reconstructed in Sekiryo Village (present-day {{ill|Tamaki, Mie|ja|玉城町}} Sekiryo) on {{ill|September 22|ja|9月22日}} (Gregorian calendar: October 22, 1663), and the deity was transferred on {{ill|December 9|ja|12月9日}} (Gregorian calendar: January 7, 1664).<ref name="sk9"/>
However, doubts arose regarding this location, and on {{ill|December 23|ja|12月23日}} of {{ill|Genroku|ja|元禄}} 4 (Gregorian calendar: February 9, 1692), Ōnakatomi Nagaharu determined that Tsumura was the original site. The shrine was relocated to its current location on {{ill|March 26|ja|3月26日}} of Genroku 7 (Gregorian calendar: April 20, 1694).<ref name="sk9"/>
The shrine was rebuilt in March {{ill|1958|ja|1958年}} (Shōwa 33), and major renovations were carried out in April {{ill|1981|ja|1981年}} (Shōwa 56).<ref name="sk1"/>

During major festivals such as {{ill|Tsukinami-sai|ja|月次祭}}, {{ill|Kannamesai|ja|神嘗祭}}, {{ill|Kinensai|ja|祈年祭}}, {{ill|Niinamesai|ja|新嘗祭}}, and temporary offerings, priests visit the shrine to conduct rituals at the shrine front.<ref name="sk0"/> Other ceremonies, such as the {{ill|Saitan-sai|ja|歳旦祭}}, are conducted remotely from the Gojōden of the Inner Shrine.<ref name="sk0"/>
Additionally, the role of {{ill|hafuri (Shinto)|ja|祝部}} is designated to carry out daily maintenance and shrine duties. The hafuri are appointed by the Jingū Administration Office.<ref name="sk0"/>
== Flora ==
[[File:Sonai-jinja 02.jpg|thumb|Sacred forest of Sonai Shrine and Mie Prefectural Route 22 Ise-Nantō Line]] The shrine forest is composed of large trees that survived the {{ill|Isewan Typhoon|ja|伊勢湾台風}}, including {{ill|Japanese cypress|ja|ヒノキ}}, {{ill|cedar|ja|スギ}}, and {{ill|Japanese black pine|ja|クロマツ}}. The shrine is located immediately nearby.<ref name="tm"/> (Approx. 7.5 km bus distance,<ref name="sk9"/> about 19 minutes.<ref name="tm"/>)
* By car, it takes about 11 minutes (approx. 7.5 km) from {{ill|Tamaki IC|ja|玉城インターチェンジ}} on the Ise Expressway, or about 27 minutes (approx. 7.3 km) from {{ill|Ise-nishi IC|ja|伊勢西インターチェンジ}}.

revision.text:
{{Shrine | Name = {{ill|Sonai Shrine|ja|園相神社}} | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = [[File:Sonai-jinja 01.jpg|280px]] | location = 723 Shiraki, Tsumura-cho, [[Ise]], [[Mie Prefecture]], Japan | Coordinates = {{coord|34|27|25.1|N|136|39|19.7|E|region:JP|display=inline}} | Map = Japan Mie | deity = * {{ill|Sonaibihiko-no-mikoto|ja|曽奈比比古命}} * {{ill|Mimae-no-kami|ja|御前神}} | type = [[Shikinaisha]] (minor rank), {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮}} auxiliary shrine (*sessha*) | Established = Unknown | Main Hall = [[Shinmei-zukuri]] | Other Names = Shiraki-san (白木さん) | festival = [[Kannamesai]] (Harvest Festival) | Rituals = }}
'''{{ill|Sonai Shrine|ja|園相神社}}''' (*Sonai Jinja*) is an auxiliary shrine (*sessha*) of {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮}} (the Inner Shrine of the [[Ise Grand Shrine]]), located in [[Ise]], [[Mie Prefecture]], Japan. It ranks third among the auxiliary shrines of the Inner Shrine.<ref>Ujiyamada City Office (1929): pp. 9–11.</ref>
The shrine enshrines deities who serve as the guardians of the sacred gardens (*御園*, *misono*) of the Grand Shrine.<ref name="gk">Gakken Publishing (2013): p. 65.</ref><ref name="ic">Ise Bunka Publishing (2008): p. 71.</ref>
== Overview ==
Located at 723 Shiraki, Tsumura-chō, {{ill|Ise, Mie|ja|伊勢市}}, {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県}}.<ref name="tm"/> This shrine is said to have been established by {{ill|Yamatohime-no-mikoto|ja|倭姫命}}.<ref name="gk"/> Locally, it is affectionately referred to as "Shiraki-san" or "Shiroki-no-miya," and has garnered deep devotion.<ref name="ic"/>
Walking through a straight path lined with tall {{ill|cedar trees|ja|杉}}, one reaches the shrine buildings.<ref name="ic"/> At the entrance of the path stands a stone inscribed in {{ill|Kyōhō|ja|享保}} (1724), prohibiting the killing of living beings.<ref name="sk1">Shikinaisha Research Association (1990): p. 221</ref> As of 1990 (Heisei 2), the shrine grounds covered 5,609 m², but 753.9 m² were transferred to Mie Prefecture for the expansion of {{ill|Mie Prefectural Route 22 Ise-Nantō Line|ja|三重県道22号伊勢南島線}}.<ref name="sk1"/> The shrine buildings are constructed in the {{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|ja|神明造}} style with board roofing, measuring approximately 9 shaku (≈2.7 m) in length, 7 shaku (≈2.1 m) in width, and 7.8 shaku (≈2.4 m) in height.<ref name="sk1"/> They are equipped with a sacred fence ({{ill|tamagaki|ja|玉垣}}), a tamagaki gate, and a {{ill|torii|ja|鳥居}}.<ref name="sk1"/>
In ancient times, there were two shrine buildings, each measuring 9 shaku (≈2.7 m) in length, 7.5 shaku (≈2.3 m) in width, and 4.6 shaku (≈1.4 m) in height.<ref name="jg">Fukuyama et al. (1975): p. 150</ref> The sacred object is a stone.<ref name="jg"/>
== Enshrined Deities ==
The enshrined deities are Sonaibihiko-no-Mikoto (曽奈比比古命) and Gozen-no-Kami (御前神).<ref name="gk"/><ref name="tm"/><ref name="ic"/> They are local deities of this region<ref name="gk"/> and protectors of the sacred gardens of Ise Grand Shrine.<ref name="ic"/> Sonaibihiko-no-Mikoto is the child of Ōminakami-no-Mikoto (大水上命) and is known as the deity of garden creation.<ref name="tm"/>
There was once a belief that the shrine could cure toothaches, and it was customary to offer 12 pairs of chopsticks made from bush clover as a token of gratitude upon recovery.<ref name="ic"/>
In ancient times, there was an independent shrine dedicated to Gozen-no-Kami, but after its reconstruction in {{ill|Bunei|ja|文永}} 3 (1266), no further reconstructions took place. Eventually, the shrine was not rebuilt, and Gozen-no-Kami came to be enshrined together with Sonaibihiko-no-Mikoto in the main shrine.<ref>Shikinaisha Research Association (1990): pp. 219–220</ref>
== History ==
It is said that when {{ill|Yamatohime-no-mikoto|ja|倭姫命}} traveled along the {{ill|Miyagawa River (Mie)|ja|宮川}}, she dedicated sacred gardens to {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神}} at this location, thus establishing the shrine.<ref name="ic"/><ref name="sk0">Shikinaisha Research Association (1990): p. 220</ref> Shrines enshrining {{ill|kunitsukami|ja|国津神}} were traditionally rebuilt using public funds, but six highly ranked shrines, including this one, were rebuilt by the shrine’s construction officials after the completion of the Inner Shrine’s transfer ceremony.<ref>Kaneko (1983): pp. 60–61</ref> Records of such a ceremony can be found in the ''Bunei 3 Naikū Sengu-ki''.<ref name="sk9">Shikinaisha Research Association (1990): p. 219</ref>
However, from around the {{ill|Ōnin era|ja|応仁}} (1467–1468), reconstruction efforts began to delay, and eventually, not only did reconstruction cease, but the location of the shrine grounds also became unknown.<ref name="sk9"/>
In {{ill|Kanbun|ja|寛文}} 3 (1663), the chief priest {{ill|Kawabe Masanaga|ja|河邊精長}} (Ōnakatomi Masanaga) conducted a search for the old shrine grounds. Referring to descriptions in the ''Kōtai Jingū Gishiki-chō'' such as "located in Numaki-go Sekiryo," the shrine was reconstructed in Sekiryo Village (present-day {{ill|Tamaki, Mie|ja|玉城町}} Sekiryo) on {{ill|September 22|ja|9月22日}} (Gregorian calendar: October 22, 1663), and the deity was transferred on {{ill|December 9|ja|12月9日}} (Gregorian calendar: January 7, 1664).<ref name="sk9"/>
However, doubts arose regarding this location, and on {{ill|December 23|ja|12月23日}} of {{ill|Genroku|ja|元禄}} 4 (Gregorian calendar: February 9, 1692), Ōnakatomi Nagaharu determined that Tsumura was the original site. The shrine was relocated to its current location on {{ill|March 26|ja|3月26日}} of Genroku 7 (Gregorian calendar: April 20, 1694).<ref name="sk9"/>
The shrine was rebuilt in March {{ill|1958|ja|1958年}} (Shōwa 33), and major renovations were carried out in April {{ill|1981|ja|1981年}} (Shōwa 56).<ref name="sk1"/>
== Rituals ==
During major festivals such as {{ill|Tsukinami-sai|ja|月次祭}}, {{ill|Kannamesai|ja|神嘗祭}}, {{ill|Kinensai|ja|祈年祭}}, {{ill|Niinamesai|ja|新嘗祭}}, and temporary offerings, priests visit the shrine to conduct rituals at the shrine front.<ref name="sk0"/> Other ceremonies, such as the {{ill|Saitan-sai|ja|歳旦祭}}, are conducted remotely from the Gojōden of the Inner Shrine.<ref name="sk0"/>
Additionally, the role of {{ill|hafuri (Shinto)|ja|祝部}} is designated to carry out daily maintenance and shrine duties. The hafuri are appointed by the Jingū Administration Office.<ref name="sk0"/>
== Flora ==
[[File:Sonai-jinja 02.jpg|thumb|Sacred forest of Sonai Shrine and Mie Prefectural Route 22 Ise-Nantō Line]] The shrine forest is composed of large trees that survived the {{ill|Isewan Typhoon|ja|伊勢湾台風}}, including {{ill|Japanese cypress|ja|ヒノキ}}, {{ill|cedar|ja|スギ}}, and {{ill|Japanese black pine|ja|クロマツ}}. It also contains a mixture of evergreen broadleaf trees such as {{ill|sakaki|ja|サカキ}}, {{ill|Japanese chinquapin|ja|シイ}}, {{ill|black oak|ja|クロバイ}}, and {{ill|Japanese cleyera|ja|ヒサカキ}}, along with deciduous broadleaf trees.<ref name="sk0"/>
== Surroundings ==
The shrine entrance faces {{ill|Mie Prefectural Route 22 Ise-Nantō Line|ja|三重県道22号伊勢南島線}}.<ref name="gk"/><ref>Ise Bunka-sha (2008): p. 70</ref> Despite its location along the roadway, the precincts remain peaceful and serene.<ref name="ic"/> Visitors coming from {{ill|Yamada (Ise)|ja|山田 (伊勢市)}} pass under the overpass of the {{ill|Ise Expressway|ja|伊勢自動車道}} to reach the shrine.<ref name="ic"/>
* From {{ill|Ise-shi Station|ja|伊勢市駅}} on the {{ill|Sangū Line|ja|参宮線}} operated by {{ill|JR Central|ja|東海旅客鉄道}} or the {{ill|Kintetsu Yamada Line|ja|近鉄山田線}} operated by {{ill|Kintetsu|ja|近畿日本鉄道}}, take a {{ill|Mie Kotsu|ja|三重交通}} bus and get off at the ''Shiraki'' bus stop. The shrine is located immediately nearby.<ref name="tm"/> (Approx. 7.5 km bus distance,<ref name="sk9"/> about 19 minutes.<ref name="tm"/>)
* By car, it takes about 11 minutes (approx. 7.5 km) from {{ill|Tamaki IC|ja|玉城インターチェンジ}} on the Ise Expressway, or about 27 minutes (approx. 7.3 km) from {{ill|Ise-nishi IC|ja|伊勢西インターチェンジ}}.
[[Category:Shinto shrines in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:Auxiliary shrines of Ise Grand Shrine]] [[Category:Sacred forests of Japan]] [[ja:園相神社]]

ISBN 978-4-900759-37-4 * 宇治山田市役所 編『宇治山田市史 上巻』宇治山田市役所、昭和4年1月20日、862p. * [[学研パブリッシング]]『伊勢神宮に行こう』Gakken Mook神社紀行セレクションvol.1、[[薗田稔]]監修、学研マーケティング、2013年7月4日、82p. ISBN 978-4-05-610047-1 * 金子延夫『玉城町史 第1巻―南伊勢の歴史と伝承―』三重県郷土資料叢書第61集、三重県郷土史料刊行会、昭和58年8月10日、276p. * 式内社研究会 編『式内社調査報告 第六巻 東海道1』[[皇學館大学]]出版部、平成2年2月28日、690p. ISBN 4-87644-080-8 * [[福山敏男]]・[[稲垣栄三|稲垣榮三]]・村瀬美樹・胡麻鶴醇之『神宮―第六十回神宮式年遷宮―』[[小学館]]、昭和50年4月20日、246p. * 『日本人なら知っておきたい 伊勢神宮と125の社』タツミムック、[[辰巳出版]]、2013年11月1日、127p. ISBN 978-4-7778-1197-7

{{Shrine | Name = {{ill|Sonai Shrine|ja|園相神社}} | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = [[File:Sonai-jinja 01.jpg|280px]] | location = 723 Shiraki, Tsumura-cho, [[Ise]], [[Mie Prefecture]], Japan | Coordinates = {{coord|34|27|25.1|N|136|39|19.7|E|region:JP|display=inline}} | Map = Japan Mie | deity = * {{ill|Sonaibihiko-no-mikoto|ja|曽奈比比古命}} * {{ill|Mimae-no-kami|ja|御前神}} | type = [[Shikinaisha]] (minor rank), {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮}} auxiliary shrine (*sessha*) | Established = Unknown | Main Hall = [[Shinmei-zukuri]] | Other Names = Shiraki-san (白木さん) | festival = [[Kannamesai]] (Harvest Festival) | Rituals = }}
'''{{ill|Sonai Shrine|ja|園相神社}}''' (*Sonai Jinja*) is an auxiliary shrine (*sessha*) of {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮}} (the Inner Shrine of the [[Ise Grand Shrine]]), located in [[Ise]], [[Mie Prefecture]], Japan. It ranks third among the auxiliary shrines of the Inner Shrine.<ref>Ujiyamada City Office (1929): pp. 9–11.</ref>
The shrine enshrines deities who serve as the guardians of the sacred gardens (*御園*, *misono*) of the Grand Shrine.<ref name="gk">Gakken Publishing (2013): p. 65.</ref><ref name="ic">Ise Bunka Publishing (2008): p. 71.</ref>
== Overview ==
Located at 723 Shiraki, Tsumura-chō, {{ill|Ise, Mie|ja|伊勢市}}, {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県}}.<ref name="tm"/> This shrine is said to have been established by {{ill|Yamatohime-no-mikoto|ja|倭姫命}}.<ref name="gk"/> Locally, it is affectionately referred to as "Shiraki-san" or "Shiroki-no-miya," and has garnered deep devotion.<ref name="ic"/>
Walking through a straight path lined with tall {{ill|cedar trees|ja|杉}}, one reaches the shrine buildings.<ref name="ic"/> At the entrance of the path stands a stone inscribed in {{ill|Kyōhō|ja|享保}} (1724), prohibiting the killing of living beings.<ref name="sk1">Shikinaisha Research Association (1990): p. 221</ref> As of 1990 (Heisei 2), the shrine grounds covered 5,609 m², but 753.9 m² were transferred to Mie Prefecture for the expansion of {{ill|Mie Prefectural Route 22 Ise-Nantō Line|ja|三重県道22号伊勢南島線}}.<ref name="sk1"/> The shrine buildings are constructed in the {{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|ja|神明造}} style with board roofing, measuring approximately 9 shaku (≈2.7 m) in length, 7 shaku (≈2.1 m) in width, and 7.8 shaku (≈2.4 m) in height.<ref name="sk1"/> They are equipped with a sacred fence ({{ill|tamagaki|ja|玉垣}}), a tamagaki gate, and a {{ill|torii|ja|鳥居}}.<ref name="sk1"/>
In ancient times, there were two shrine buildings, each measuring 9 shaku (≈2.7 m) in length, 7.5 shaku (≈2.3 m) in width, and 4.6 shaku (≈1.4 m) in height.<ref name="jg">Fukuyama et al. (1975): p. 150</ref> The sacred object is a stone.<ref name="jg"/>
== Enshrined Deities ==
The enshrined deities are Sonaibihiko-no-Mikoto (曽奈比比古命) and Gozen-no-Kami (御前神).<ref name="gk"/><ref name="tm"/><ref name="ic"/> They are local deities of this region<ref name="gk"/> and protectors of the sacred gardens of Ise Grand Shrine.<ref name="ic"/> Sonaibihiko-no-Mikoto is the child of Ōminakami-no-Mikoto (大水上命) and is known as the deity of garden creation.<ref name="tm"/>
There was once a belief that the shrine could cure toothaches, and it was customary to offer 12 pairs of chopsticks made from bush clover as a token of gratitude upon recovery.<ref name="ic"/>
In ancient times, there was an independent shrine dedicated to Gozen-no-Kami, but after its reconstruction in {{ill|Bunei|ja|文永}} 3 (1266), no further reconstructions took place. Eventually, the shrine was not rebuilt, and Gozen-no-Kami came to be enshrined together with Sonaibihiko-no-Mikoto in the main shrine.<ref>Shikinaisha Research Association (1990): pp. 219–220</ref>
== History ==
It is said that when {{ill|Yamatohime-no-mikoto|ja|倭姫命}} traveled along the {{ill|Miyagawa River (Mie)|ja|宮川}}, she dedicated sacred gardens to {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神}} at this location, thus establishing the shrine.<ref name="ic"/><ref name="sk0">Shikinaisha Research Association (1990): p. 220</ref> Shrines enshrining {{ill|kunitsukami|ja|国津神}} were traditionally rebuilt using public funds, but six highly ranked shrines, including this one, were rebuilt by the shrine’s construction officials after the completion of the Inner Shrine’s transfer ceremony.<ref>Kaneko (1983): pp. 60–61</ref> Records of such a ceremony can be found in the ''Bunei 3 Naikū Sengu-ki''.<ref name="sk9">Shikinaisha Research Association (1990): p. 219</ref>
However, from around the {{ill|Ōnin era|ja|応仁}} (1467–1468), reconstruction efforts began to delay, and eventually, not only did reconstruction cease, but the location of the shrine grounds also became unknown.<ref name="sk9"/>
In {{ill|Kanbun|ja|寛文}} 3 (1663), the chief priest {{ill|Kawabe Masanaga|ja|河邊精長}} (Ōnakatomi Masanaga) conducted a search for the old shrine grounds. Referring to descriptions in the ''Kōtai Jingū Gishiki-chō'' such as "located in Numaki-go Sekiryo," the shrine was reconstructed in Sekiryo Village (present-day {{ill|Tamaki, Mie|ja|玉城町}} Sekiryo) on {{ill|September 22|ja|9月22日}} (Gregorian calendar: October 22, 1663), and the deity was transferred on {{ill|December 9|ja|12月9日}} (Gregorian calendar: January 7, 1664).<ref name="sk9"/>
However, doubts arose regarding this location, and on {{ill|December 23|ja|12月23日}} of {{ill|Genroku|ja|元禄}} 4 (Gregorian calendar: February 9, 1692), Ōnakatomi Nagaharu determined that Tsumura was the original site. The shrine was relocated to its current location on {{ill|March 26|ja|3月26日}} of Genroku 7 (Gregorian calendar: April 20, 1694).<ref name="sk9"/>
The shrine was rebuilt in March {{ill|1958|ja|1958年}} (Shōwa 33), and major renovations were carried out in April {{ill|1981|ja|1981年}} (Shōwa 56).<ref name="sk1"/>
== Rituals ==
During major festivals such as {{ill|Tsukinami-sai|ja|月次祭}}, {{ill|Kannamesai|ja|神嘗祭}}, {{ill|Kinensai|ja|祈年祭}}, {{ill|Niinamesai|ja|新嘗祭}}, and temporary offerings, priests visit the shrine to conduct rituals at the shrine front.<ref name="sk0"/> Other ceremonies, such as the {{ill|Saitan-sai|ja|歳旦祭}}, are conducted remotely from the Gojōden of the Inner Shrine.<ref name="sk0"/>
Additionally, the role of {{ill|hafuri (Shinto)|ja|祝部}} is designated to carry out daily maintenance and shrine duties. The hafuri are appointed by the Jingū Administration Office.<ref name="sk0"/>
== Flora ==
[[File:Sonai-jinja 02.jpg|thumb|Sacred forest of Sonai Shrine and Mie Prefectural Route 22 Ise-Nantō Line]] The shrine forest is composed of large trees that survived the {{ill|Isewan Typhoon|ja|伊勢湾台風}}, including {{ill|Japanese cypress|ja|ヒノキ}}, {{ill|cedar|ja|スギ}}, and {{ill|Japanese black pine|ja|クロマツ}}. It also contains a mixture of evergreen broadleaf trees such as {{ill|sakaki|ja|サカキ}}, {{ill|Japanese chinquapin|ja|シイ}}, {{ill|black oak|ja|クロバイ}}, and {{ill|Japanese cleyera|ja|ヒサカキ}}, along with deciduous broadleaf trees.<ref name="sk0"/>
== Surroundings ==
The shrine entrance faces {{ill|Mie Prefectural Route 22 Ise-Nantō Line|ja|三重県道22号伊勢南島線}}.<ref name="gk"/><ref>Ise Bunka-sha (2008): p. 70</ref> Despite its location along the roadway, the precincts remain peaceful and serene.<ref name="ic"/> Visitors coming from {{ill|Yamada (Ise)|ja|山田 (伊勢市)}} pass under the overpass of the {{ill|Ise Expressway|ja|伊勢自動車道}} to reach the shrine.<ref name="ic"/>
* From {{ill|Ise-shi Station|ja|伊勢市駅}} on the {{ill|Sangū Line|ja|参宮線}} operated by {{ill|JR Central|ja|東海旅客鉄道}} or the {{ill|Kintetsu Yamada Line|ja|近鉄山田線}} operated by {{ill|Kintetsu|ja|近畿日本鉄道}}, take a {{ill|Mie Kotsu|ja|三重交通}} bus and get off at the ''Shiraki'' bus stop. The shrine is located immediately nearby.<ref name="tm"/> (Approx. 7.5 km bus distance,<ref name="sk9"/> about 19 minutes.<ref name="tm"/>)
* By car, it takes about 11 minutes (approx. 7.5 km) from {{ill|Tamaki IC|ja|玉城インターチェンジ}} on the Ise Expressway, or about 27 minutes (approx. 7.3 km) from {{ill|Ise-nishi IC|ja|伊勢西インターチェンジ}}.

Eventually, the shrine was not rebuilt, and Gozen-no-Kami came to be enshrined together with Sonaibihiko-no-Mikoto in the main shrine.<ref>Shikinaisha Research Association (1990): pp. 219–220</ref>

{{Shrine | Name = {{ill|Sonai Shrine|ja|園相神社}} | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = [[File:Sonai-jinja 01.jpg|280px]] | location = 723 Shiraki, Tsumura-cho, [[Ise]], [[Mie Prefecture]], Japan | coordinates = {{coord|34|27|25.1|N|136|39|19.7|E|region:JP|display=inline}} | Map = Japan Mie | deity = * {{ill|Sonaibihiko-no-mikoto|ja|曽奈比比古命}} * {{ill|Mimae-no-kami|ja|御前神}} | type = [[Shikinaisha]] (minor rank), {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮}} auxiliary shrine (*sessha*) | Established = Unknown | Main Hall = [[Shinmei-zukuri]] | Other Names = Shiraki-san (白木さん) | festival = [[Kannamesai]] (Harvest Festival) | Rituals = }}
'''{{ill|Sonai Shrine|ja|園相神社}}''' (*Sonai Jinja*) is an auxiliary shrine (*sessha*) of {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮}} (the Inner Shrine of the [[Ise Grand Shrine]]), located in [[Ise]], [[Mie Prefecture]], Japan. It ranks third among the auxiliary shrines of the Inner Shrine.<ref>Ujiyamada City Office (1929): pp. 9–11.</ref>
The shrine enshrines deities who serve as the guardians of the sacred gardens (*御園*, *misono*) of the Grand Shrine.<ref name="gk">Gakken Publishing (2013): p. 65.</ref><ref name="ic">Ise Bunka Publishing (2008): p. 71.</ref>
== Overview ==
Located at 723 Shiraki, Tsumura-chō, {{ill|Ise, Mie|ja|伊勢市}}, {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県}}.<ref name="tm"/> This shrine is said to have been established by {{ill|Yamatohime-no-mikoto|ja|倭姫命}}.<ref name="gk"/> Locally, it is affectionately referred to as "Shiraki-san" or "Shiroki-no-miya," and has garnered deep devotion.<ref name="ic"/>
Walking through a straight path lined with tall {{ill|cedar trees|ja|杉}}, one reaches the shrine buildings.<ref name="ic"/> At the entrance of the path stands a stone inscribed in {{ill|Kyōhō|ja|享保}} (1724), prohibiting the killing of living beings.<ref name="sk1">Shikinaisha Research Association (1990): p. 221</ref> As of 1990 (Heisei 2), the shrine grounds covered 5,609 m², but 753.9 m² were transferred to Mie Prefecture for the expansion of {{ill|Mie Prefectural Route 22 Ise-Nantō Line|ja|三重県道22号伊勢南島線}}.<ref name="sk1"/> The shrine buildings are constructed in the {{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|ja|神明造}} style with board roofing, measuring approximately 9 shaku (≈2.7 m) in length, 7 shaku (≈2.1 m) in width, and 7.8 shaku (≈2.4 m) in height.<ref name="sk1"/> They are equipped with a sacred fence ({{ill|tamagaki|ja|玉垣}}), a tamagaki gate, and a {{ill|torii|ja|鳥居}}.<ref name="sk1"/>
In ancient times, there were two shrine buildings, each measuring 9 shaku (≈2.7 m) in length, 7.5 shaku (≈2.3 m) in width, and 4.6 shaku (≈1.4 m) in height.<ref name="jg">Fukuyama et al. (1975): p. 150</ref> The sacred object is a stone.<ref name="jg"/>
== Enshrined Deities ==
The enshrined deities are Sonaibihiko-no-Mikoto (曽奈比比古命) and Gozen-no-Kami (御前神).<ref name="gk"/><ref name="tm"/><ref name="ic"/> They are local deities of this region<ref name="gk"/> and protectors of the sacred gardens of Ise Grand Shrine.<ref name="ic"/> Sonaibihiko-no-Mikoto is the child of Ōminakami-no-Mikoto (大水上命) and is known as the deity of garden creation.<ref name="tm"/>
There was once a belief that the shrine could cure toothaches, and it was customary to offer 12 pairs of chopsticks made from bush clover as a token of gratitude upon recovery.<ref name="ic"/>
In ancient times, there was an independent shrine dedicated to Gozen-no-Kami, but after its reconstruction in {{ill|Bunei|ja|文永}} 3 (1266), no further reconstructions took place. Eventually, the shrine was not rebuilt, and Gozen-no-Kami came to be enshrined together with Sonaibihiko-no-Mikoto in the main shrine.<ref>Shikinaisha Research Association (1990): pp. 219–220</ref>
== History ==
It is said that when {{ill|Yamatohime-no-mikoto|ja|倭姫命}} traveled along the {{ill|Miyagawa River (Mie)|ja|宮川}}, she dedicated sacred gardens to {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神}} at this location, thus establishing the shrine.<ref name="ic"/><ref name="sk0">Shikinaisha Research Association (1990): p. 220</ref> Shrines enshrining {{ill|kunitsukami|ja|国津神}} were traditionally rebuilt using public funds, but six highly ranked shrines, including this one, were rebuilt by the shrine’s construction officials after the completion of the Inner Shrine’s transfer ceremony.<ref>Kaneko (1983): pp. 60–61</ref> Records of such a ceremony can be found in the ''Bunei 3 Naikū Sengu-ki''.<ref name="sk9">Shikinaisha Research Association (1990): p. 219</ref>
However, from around the {{ill|Ōnin era|ja|応仁}} (1467–1468), reconstruction efforts began to delay, and eventually, not only did reconstruction cease, but the location of the shrine grounds also became unknown.<ref name="sk9"/>
In {{ill|Kanbun|ja|寛文}} 3 (1663), the chief priest {{ill|Kawabe Masanaga|ja|河邊精長}} (Ōnakatomi Masanaga) conducted a search for the old shrine grounds. Referring to descriptions in the ''Kōtai Jingū Gishiki-chō'' such as "located in Numaki-go Sekiryo," the shrine was reconstructed in Sekiryo Village (present-day {{ill|Tamaki, Mie|ja|玉城町}} Sekiryo) on {{ill|September 22|ja|9月22日}} (Gregorian calendar: October 22, 1663), and the deity was transferred on {{ill|December 9|ja|12月9日}} (Gregorian calendar: January 7, 1664).<ref name="sk9"/>
However, doubts arose regarding this location, and on {{ill|December 23|ja|12月23日}} of {{ill|Genroku|ja|元禄}} 4 (Gregorian calendar: February 9, 1692), Ōnakatomi Nagaharu determined that Tsumura was the original site. The shrine was relocated to its current location on {{ill|March 26|ja|3月26日}} of Genroku 7 (Gregorian calendar: April 20, 1694).<ref name="sk9"/>
The shrine was rebuilt in March {{ill|1958|ja|1958年}} (Shōwa 33), and major renovations were carried out in April {{ill|1981|ja|1981年}} (Shōwa 56).<ref name="sk1"/>
== Rituals ==
During major festivals such as {{ill|Tsukinami-sai|ja|月次祭}}, {{ill|Kannamesai|ja|神嘗祭}}, {{ill|Kinensai|ja|祈年祭}}, {{ill|Niinamesai|ja|新嘗祭}}, and temporary offerings, priests visit the shrine to conduct rituals at the shrine front.<ref name="sk0"/> Other ceremonies, such as the {{ill|Saitan-sai|ja|歳旦祭}}, are conducted remotely from the Gojōden of the Inner Shrine.<ref name="sk0"/>
Additionally, the role of {{ill|hafuri (Shinto)|ja|祝部}} is designated to carry out daily maintenance and shrine duties. The hafuri are appointed by the Jingū Administration Office.<ref name="sk0"/>
== Flora ==
[[File:Sonai-jinja 02.jpg|thumb|Sacred forest of Sonai Shrine and Mie Prefectural Route 22 Ise-Nantō Line]] The shrine forest is composed of large trees that survived the {{ill|Isewan Typhoon|ja|伊勢湾台風}}, including {{ill|Japanese cypress|ja|ヒノキ}}, {{ill|cedar|ja|スギ}}, and {{ill|Japanese black pine|ja|クロマツ}}. It also contains a mixture of evergreen broadleaf trees such as {{ill|sakaki|ja|サカキ}}, {{ill|Japanese chinquapin|ja|シイ}}, {{ill|black oak|ja|クロバイ}}, and {{ill|Japanese cleyera|ja|ヒサカキ}}, along with deciduous broadleaf trees.<ref name="sk0"/>
== Surroundings ==
The shrine entrance faces {{ill|Mie Prefectural Route 22 Ise-Nantō Line|ja|三重県道22号伊勢南島線}}.<ref name="gk"/><ref>Ise Bunka-sha (2008): p. 70</ref> Despite its location along the roadway, the precincts remain peaceful and serene.<ref name="ic"/> Visitors coming from {{ill|Yamada (Ise)|ja|山田 (伊勢市)}} pass under the overpass of the {{ill|Ise Expressway|ja|伊勢自動車道}} to reach the shrine.<ref name="ic"/>
* From {{ill|Ise-shi Station|ja|伊勢市駅}} on the {{ill|Sangū Line|ja|参宮線}} operated by {{ill|JR Central|ja|東海旅客鉄道}} or the {{ill|Kintetsu Yamada Line|ja|近鉄山田線}} operated by {{ill|Kintetsu|ja|近畿日本鉄道}}, take a {{ill|Mie Kotsu|ja|三重交通}} bus and get off at the ''Shiraki'' bus stop. The shrine is located immediately nearby.<ref name="tm"/> (Approx. 7.5 km bus distance,<ref name="sk9"/> about 19 minutes.<ref name="tm"/>)
* By car, it takes about 11 minutes (approx. 7.5 km) from {{ill|Tamaki IC|ja|玉城インターチェンジ}} on the Ise Expressway, or about 27 minutes (approx. 7.3 km) from {{ill|Ise-nishi IC|ja|伊勢西インターチェンジ}}.

revision.text:
{{Shrine | Name = {{ill|Sonai Shrine|ja|園相神社}} | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = [[File:Sonai-jinja 01.jpg|280px]] | location = 723 Shiraki, Tsumura-cho, [[Ise]], [[Mie Prefecture]], Japan | coordinates = {{coord|34|27|25.1|N|136|39|19.7|E|region:JP|display=inline}} | Map = Japan Mie | deity = * {{ill|Sonaibihiko-no-mikoto|ja|曽奈比比古命}} * {{ill|Mimae-no-kami|ja|御前神}} | type = [[Shikinaisha]] (minor rank), {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮}} auxiliary shrine (*sessha*) | Established = Unknown | Main Hall = [[Shinmei-zukuri]] | Other Names = Shiraki-san (白木さん) | festival = [[Kannamesai]] (Harvest Festival) | Rituals = }}
'''{{ill|Sonai Shrine|ja|園相神社}}''' (*Sonai Jinja*) is an auxiliary shrine (*sessha*) of {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮}} (the Inner Shrine of the [[Ise Grand Shrine]]), located in [[Ise]], [[Mie Prefecture]], Japan. It ranks third among the auxiliary shrines of the Inner Shrine.<ref>Ujiyamada City Office (1929): pp. 9–11.</ref>
The shrine enshrines deities who serve as the guardians of the sacred gardens (*御園*, *misono*) of the Grand Shrine.<ref name="gk">Gakken Publishing (2013): p. 65.</ref><ref name="ic">Ise Bunka Publishing (2008): p. 71.</ref>
== Overview ==
Located at 723 Shiraki, Tsumura-chō, {{ill|Ise, Mie|ja|伊勢市}}, {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県}}.<ref name="tm"/> This shrine is said to have been established by {{ill|Yamatohime-no-mikoto|ja|倭姫命}}.<ref name="gk"/> Locally, it is affectionately referred to as "Shiraki-san" or "Shiroki-no-miya," and has garnered deep devotion.<ref name="ic"/>
Walking through a straight path lined with tall {{ill|cedar trees|ja|杉}}, one reaches the shrine buildings.<ref name="ic"/> At the entrance of the path stands a stone inscribed in {{ill|Kyōhō|ja|享保}} (1724), prohibiting the killing of living beings.<ref name="sk1">Shikinaisha Research Association (1990): p. 221</ref> As of 1990 (Heisei 2), the shrine grounds covered 5,609 m², but 753.9 m² were transferred to Mie Prefecture for the expansion of {{ill|Mie Prefectural Route 22 Ise-Nantō Line|ja|三重県道22号伊勢南島線}}.<ref name="sk1"/> The shrine buildings are constructed in the {{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|ja|神明造}} style with board roofing, measuring approximately 9 shaku (≈2.7 m) in length, 7 shaku (≈2.1 m) in width, and 7.8 shaku (≈2.4 m) in height.<ref name="sk1"/> They are equipped with a sacred fence ({{ill|tamagaki|ja|玉垣}}), a tamagaki gate, and a {{ill|torii|ja|鳥居}}.<ref name="sk1"/>
In ancient times, there were two shrine buildings, each measuring 9 shaku (≈2.7 m) in length, 7.5 shaku (≈2.3 m) in width, and 4.6 shaku (≈1.4 m) in height.<ref name="jg">Fukuyama et al. (1975): p. 150</ref> The sacred object is a stone.<ref name="jg"/>
== Enshrined Deities ==
The enshrined deities are Sonaibihiko-no-Mikoto (曽奈比比古命) and Gozen-no-Kami (御前神).<ref name="gk"/><ref name="tm"/><ref name="ic"/> They are local deities of this region<ref name="gk"/> and protectors of the sacred gardens of Ise Grand Shrine.<ref name="ic"/> Sonaibihiko-no-Mikoto is the child of Ōminakami-no-Mikoto (大水上命) and is known as the deity of garden creation.<ref name="tm"/>
There was once a belief that the shrine could cure toothaches, and it was customary to offer 12 pairs of chopsticks made from bush clover as a token of gratitude upon recovery.<ref name="ic"/>
In ancient times, there was an independent shrine dedicated to Gozen-no-Kami, but after its reconstruction in {{ill|Bunei|ja|文永}} 3 (1266), no further reconstructions took place. Eventually, the shrine was not rebuilt, and Gozen-no-Kami came to be enshrined together with Sonaibihiko-no-Mikoto in the main shrine.<ref>Shikinaisha Research Association (1990): pp. 219–220</ref>
== History ==
It is said that when {{ill|Yamatohime-no-mikoto|ja|倭姫命}} traveled along the {{ill|Miyagawa River (Mie)|ja|宮川}}, she dedicated sacred gardens to {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神}} at this location, thus establishing the shrine.<ref name="ic"/><ref name="sk0">Shikinaisha Research Association (1990): p. 220</ref> Shrines enshrining {{ill|kunitsukami|ja|国津神}} were traditionally rebuilt using public funds, but six highly ranked shrines, including this one, were rebuilt by the shrine’s construction officials after the completion of the Inner Shrine’s transfer ceremony.<ref>Kaneko (1983): pp. 60–61</ref> Records of such a ceremony can be found in the ''Bunei 3 Naikū Sengu-ki''.<ref name="sk9">Shikinaisha Research Association (1990): p. 219</ref>
However, from around the {{ill|Ōnin era|ja|応仁}} (1467–1468), reconstruction efforts began to delay, and eventually, not only did reconstruction cease, but the location of the shrine grounds also became unknown.<ref name="sk9"/>
In {{ill|Kanbun|ja|寛文}} 3 (1663), the chief priest {{ill|Kawabe Masanaga|ja|河邊精長}} (Ōnakatomi Masanaga) conducted a search for the old shrine grounds. Referring to descriptions in the ''Kōtai Jingū Gishiki-chō'' such as "located in Numaki-go Sekiryo," the shrine was reconstructed in Sekiryo Village (present-day {{ill|Tamaki, Mie|ja|玉城町}} Sekiryo) on {{ill|September 22|ja|9月22日}} (Gregorian calendar: October 22, 1663), and the deity was transferred on {{ill|December 9|ja|12月9日}} (Gregorian calendar: January 7, 1664).<ref name="sk9"/>
However, doubts arose regarding this location, and on {{ill|December 23|ja|12月23日}} of {{ill|Genroku|ja|元禄}} 4 (Gregorian calendar: February 9, 1692), Ōnakatomi Nagaharu determined that Tsumura was the original site. The shrine was relocated to its current location on {{ill|March 26|ja|3月26日}} of Genroku 7 (Gregorian calendar: April 20, 1694).<ref name="sk9"/>
The shrine was rebuilt in March {{ill|1958|ja|1958年}} (Shōwa 33), and major renovations were carried out in April {{ill|1981|ja|1981年}} (Shōwa 56).<ref name="sk1"/>
== Rituals ==
During major festivals such as {{ill|Tsukinami-sai|ja|月次祭}}, {{ill|Kannamesai|ja|神嘗祭}}, {{ill|Kinensai|ja|祈年祭}}, {{ill|Niinamesai|ja|新嘗祭}}, and temporary offerings, priests visit the shrine to conduct rituals at the shrine front.<ref name="sk0"/> Other ceremonies, such as the {{ill|Saitan-sai|ja|歳旦祭}}, are conducted remotely from the Gojōden of the Inner Shrine.<ref name="sk0"/>
Additionally, the role of {{ill|hafuri (Shinto)|ja|祝部}} is designated to carry out daily maintenance and shrine duties. The hafuri are appointed by the Jingū Administration Office.<ref name="sk0"/>
== Flora ==
[[File:Sonai-jinja 02.jpg|thumb|Sacred forest of Sonai Shrine and Mie Prefectural Route 22 Ise-Nantō Line]] The shrine forest is composed of large trees that survived the {{ill|Isewan Typhoon|ja|伊勢湾台風}}, including {{ill|Japanese cypress|ja|ヒノキ}}, {{ill|cedar|ja|スギ}}, and {{ill|Japanese black pine|ja|クロマツ}}. It also contains a mixture of evergreen broadleaf trees such as {{ill|sakaki|ja|サカキ}}, {{ill|Japanese chinquapin|ja|シイ}}, {{ill|black oak|ja|クロバイ}}, and {{ill|Japanese cleyera|ja|ヒサカキ}}, along with deciduous broadleaf trees.<ref name="sk0"/>
== Surroundings ==
The shrine entrance faces {{ill|Mie Prefectural Route 22 Ise-Nantō Line|ja|三重県道22号伊勢南島線}}.<ref name="gk"/><ref>Ise Bunka-sha (2008): p. 70</ref> Despite its location along the roadway, the precincts remain peaceful and serene.<ref name="ic"/> Visitors coming from {{ill|Yamada (Ise)|ja|山田 (伊勢市)}} pass under the overpass of the {{ill|Ise Expressway|ja|伊勢自動車道}} to reach the shrine.<ref name="ic"/>
* From {{ill|Ise-shi Station|ja|伊勢市駅}} on the {{ill|Sangū Line|ja|参宮線}} operated by {{ill|JR Central|ja|東海旅客鉄道}} or the {{ill|Kintetsu Yamada Line|ja|近鉄山田線}} operated by {{ill|Kintetsu|ja|近畿日本鉄道}}, take a {{ill|Mie Kotsu|ja|三重交通}} bus and get off at the ''Shiraki'' bus stop. The shrine is located immediately nearby.<ref name="tm"/> (Approx. 7.5 km bus distance,<ref name="sk9"/> about 19 minutes.<ref name="tm"/>)
* By car, it takes about 11 minutes (approx. 7.5 km) from {{ill|Tamaki IC|ja|玉城インターチェンジ}} on the Ise Expressway, or about 27 minutes (approx. 7.3 km) from {{ill|Ise-nishi IC|ja|伊勢西インターチェンジ}}.
[[Category:Shinto shrines in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:Auxiliary shrines of Ise Grand Shrine]] [[Category:Sacred forests of Japan]] [[ja:園相神社]]

ISBN 978-4-900759-37-4 * 宇治山田市役所 編『宇治山田市史 上巻』宇治山田市役所、昭和4年1月20日、862p. * [[学研パブリッシング]]『伊勢神宮に行こう』Gakken Mook神社紀行セレクションvol.1、[[薗田稔]]監修、学研マーケティング、2013年7月4日、82p. ISBN 978-4-05-610047-1 * 金子延夫『玉城町史 第1巻―南伊勢の歴史と伝承―』三重県郷土資料叢書第61集、三重県郷土史料刊行会、昭和58年8月10日、276p. * 式内社研究会 編『式内社調査報告 第六巻 東海道1』[[皇學館大学]]出版部、平成2年2月28日、690p. ISBN 4-87644-080-8 * [[福山敏男]]・[[稲垣栄三|稲垣榮三]]・村瀬美樹・胡麻鶴醇之『神宮―第六十回神宮式年遷宮―』[[小学館]]、昭和50年4月20日、246p. * 『日本人なら知っておきたい 伊勢神宮と125の社』タツミムック、[[辰巳出版]]、2013年11月1日、127p. ISBN 978-4-7778-1197-7

revision.text:
{{Shrine | Name = {{ill|Sonai Shrine|ja|園相神社}} | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = [[File:Sonai-jinja 01.jpg|280px]] | location = 723 Shiraki, Tsumura-cho, [[Ise]], [[Mie Prefecture]], Japan | coordinates = {{coord|34|27|25.1|N|136|39|19.7|E|region:JP|display=inline}} | Map = Japan Mie | deity = * {{ill|Sonaibihiko-no-mikoto|ja|曽奈比比古命}} * {{ill|Mimae-no-kami|ja|御前神}} | type = [[Shikinaisha]] (minor rank), {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮}} auxiliary shrine (*sessha*) | Established = Unknown | Main Hall = [[Shinmei-zukuri]] | Other Names = Shiraki-san (白木さん) | festival = [[Kannamesai]] (Harvest Festival) | Rituals = }}
'''{{ill|Sonai Shrine|ja|園相神社}}''' (*Sonai Jinja*) is an auxiliary shrine (*sessha*) of {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮}} (the Inner Shrine of the [[Ise Grand Shrine]]), located in [[Ise]], [[Mie Prefecture]], Japan. It ranks third among the auxiliary shrines of the Inner Shrine.<ref>Ujiyamada City Office (1929): pp. 9–11.</ref>
The shrine enshrines deities who serve as the guardians of the sacred gardens (*御園*, *misono*) of the Grand Shrine.<ref name="gk">Gakken Publishing (2013): p. 65.</ref><ref name="ic">Ise Bunka Publishing (2008): p. 71.</ref>
== Overview ==
Located at 723 Shiraki, Tsumura-chō, {{ill|Ise, Mie|ja|伊勢市}}, {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県}}.<ref name="tm"/> This shrine is said to have been established by {{ill|Yamatohime-no-mikoto|ja|倭姫命}}.<ref name="gk"/> Locally, it is affectionately referred to as "Shiraki-san" or "Shiroki-no-miya," and has garnered deep devotion.<ref name="ic"/>
Walking through a straight path lined with tall {{ill|cedar trees|ja|杉}}, one reaches the shrine buildings.<ref name="ic"/> At the entrance of the path stands a stone inscribed in {{ill|Kyōhō|ja|享保}} (1724), prohibiting the killing of living beings.<ref name="sk1">Shikinaisha Research Association (1990): p. 221</ref> As of 1990 (Heisei 2), the shrine grounds covered 5,609 m², but 753.9 m² were transferred to Mie Prefecture for the expansion of {{ill|Mie Prefectural Route 22 Ise-Nantō Line|ja|三重県道22号伊勢南島線}}.<ref name="sk1"/> The shrine buildings are constructed in the {{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|ja|神明造}} style with board roofing, measuring approximately 9 shaku (≈2.7 m) in length, 7 shaku (≈2.1 m) in width, and 7.8 shaku (≈2.4 m) in height.<ref name="sk1"/> They are equipped with a sacred fence ({{ill|tamagaki|ja|玉垣}}), a tamagaki gate, and a {{ill|torii|ja|鳥居}}.<ref name="sk1"/>
In ancient times, there were two shrine buildings, each measuring 9 shaku (≈2.7 m) in length, 7.5 shaku (≈2.3 m) in width, and 4.6 shaku (≈1.4 m) in height.<ref name="jg">Fukuyama et al. (1975): p. 150</ref> The sacred object is a stone.<ref name="jg"/>
== Enshrined Deities ==
The enshrined deities are Sonaibihiko-no-Mikoto (曽奈比比古命) and Gozen-no-Kami (御前神).<ref name="gk"/><ref name="tm"/><ref name="ic"/> They are local deities of this region<ref name="gk"/> and protectors of the sacred gardens of Ise Grand Shrine.<ref name="ic"/> Sonaibihiko-no-Mikoto is the child of Ōminakami-no-Mikoto (大水上命) and is known as the deity of garden creation.<ref name="tm"/>
There was once a belief that the shrine could cure toothaches, and it was customary to offer 12 pairs of chopsticks made from bush clover as a token of gratitude upon recovery.<ref name="ic"/>
In ancient times, there was an independent shrine dedicated to Gozen-no-Kami, but after its reconstruction in {{ill|Bunei|ja|文永}} 3 (1266), no further reconstructions took place. Eventually, the shrine was not rebuilt, and Gozen-no-Kami came to be enshrined together with Sonaibihiko-no-Mikoto in the main shrine.<ref>Shikinaisha Research Association (1990): pp. 219–220</ref>
== History ==
It is said that when {{ill|Yamatohime-no-mikoto|ja|倭姫命}} traveled along the {{ill|Miyagawa River (Mie)|ja|宮川}}, she dedicated sacred gardens to {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神}} at this location, thus establishing the shrine.<ref name="ic"/><ref name="sk0">Shikinaisha Research Association (1990): p. 220</ref> Shrines enshrining {{ill|kunitsukami|ja|国津神}} were traditionally rebuilt using public funds, but six highly ranked shrines, including this one, were rebuilt by the shrine’s construction officials after the completion of the Inner Shrine’s transfer ceremony.<ref>Kaneko (1983): pp. 60–61</ref> Records of such a ceremony can be found in the ''Bunei 3 Naikū Sengu-ki''.<ref name="sk9">Shikinaisha Research Association (1990): p. 219</ref>
However, from around the {{ill|Ōnin era|ja|応仁}} (1467–1468), reconstruction efforts began to delay, and eventually, not only did reconstruction cease, but the location of the shrine grounds also became unknown.<ref name="sk9"/>
In {{ill|Kanbun|ja|寛文}} 3 (1663), the chief priest {{ill|Kawabe Masanaga|ja|河邊精長}} (Ōnakatomi Masanaga) conducted a search for the old shrine grounds. Referring to descriptions in the ''Kōtai Jingū Gishiki-chō'' such as "located in Numaki-go Sekiryo," the shrine was reconstructed in Sekiryo Village (present-day {{ill|Tamaki, Mie|ja|玉城町}} Sekiryo) on {{ill|September 22|ja|9月22日}} (Gregorian calendar: October 22, 1663), and the deity was transferred on {{ill|December 9|ja|12月9日}} (Gregorian calendar: January 7, 1664).<ref name="sk9"/>
However, doubts arose regarding this location, and on {{ill|December 23|ja|12月23日}} of {{ill|Genroku|ja|元禄}} 4 (Gregorian calendar: February 9, 1692), Ōnakatomi Nagaharu determined that Tsumura was the original site. The shrine was relocated to its current location on {{ill|March 26|ja|3月26日}} of Genroku 7 (Gregorian calendar: April 20, 1694).<ref name="sk9"/>
The shrine was rebuilt in March {{ill|1958|ja|1958年}} (Shōwa 33), and major renovations were carried out in April {{ill|1981|ja|1981年}} (Shōwa 56).<ref name="sk1"/>
== Rituals ==
During major festivals such as {{ill|Tsukinami-sai|ja|月次祭}}, {{ill|Kannamesai|ja|神嘗祭}}, {{ill|Kinensai|ja|祈年祭}}, {{ill|Niinamesai|ja|新嘗祭}}, and temporary offerings, priests visit the shrine to conduct rituals at the shrine front.<ref name="sk0"/> Other ceremonies, such as the {{ill|Saitan-sai|ja|歳旦祭}}, are conducted remotely from the Gojōden of the Inner Shrine.<ref name="sk0"/>
Additionally, the role of {{ill|hafuri (Shinto)|ja|祝部}} is designated to carry out daily maintenance and shrine duties. The hafuri are appointed by the Jingū Administration Office.<ref name="sk0"/>
== Flora ==
[[File:Sonai-jinja 02.jpg|thumb|Sacred forest of Sonai Shrine and Mie Prefectural Route 22 Ise-Nantō Line]] The shrine forest is composed of large trees that survived the {{ill|Isewan Typhoon|ja|伊勢湾台風}}, including {{ill|Japanese cypress|ja|ヒノキ}}, {{ill|cedar|ja|スギ}}, and {{ill|Japanese black pine|ja|クロマツ}}. It also contains a mixture of evergreen broadleaf trees such as {{ill|sakaki|ja|サカキ}}, {{ill|Japanese chinquapin|ja|シイ}}, {{ill|black oak|ja|クロバイ}}, and {{ill|Japanese cleyera|ja|ヒサカキ}}, along with deciduous broadleaf trees.<ref name="sk0"/>
== Surroundings ==
The shrine entrance faces {{ill|Mie Prefectural Route 22 Ise-Nantō Line|ja|三重県道22号伊勢南島線}}.<ref name="gk"/><ref>Ise Bunka-sha (2008): p. 70</ref> Despite its location along the roadway, the precincts remain peaceful and serene.<ref name="ic"/> Visitors coming from {{ill|Yamada (Ise)|ja|山田 (伊勢市)}} pass under the overpass of the {{ill|Ise Expressway|ja|伊勢自動車道}} to reach the shrine.<ref name="ic"/>
* From {{ill|Ise-shi Station|ja|伊勢市駅}} on the {{ill|Sangū Line|ja|参宮線}} operated by {{ill|JR Central|ja|東海旅客鉄道}} or the {{ill|Kintetsu Yamada Line|ja|近鉄山田線}} operated by {{ill|Kintetsu|ja|近畿日本鉄道}}, take a {{ill|Mie Kotsu|ja|三重交通}} bus and get off at the ''Shiraki'' bus stop. The shrine is located immediately nearby.<ref name="tm"/> (Approx. 7.5 km bus distance,<ref name="sk9"/> about 19 minutes.<ref name="tm"/>)
* By car, it takes about 11 minutes (approx. 7.5 km) from {{ill|Tamaki IC|ja|玉城インターチェンジ}} on the Ise Expressway, or about 27 minutes (approx. 7.3 km) from {{ill|Ise-nishi IC|ja|伊勢西インターチェンジ}}.
[[Category:Shinto shrines in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:Auxiliary shrines of Ise Grand Shrine]] [[Category:Sacred forests of Japan]] [[ja:園相神社]]

ISBN 978-4-900759-37-4 * 宇治山田市役所 編『宇治山田市史 上巻』宇治山田市役所、昭和4年1月20日、862p. * [[学研パブリッシング]]『伊勢神宮に行こう』Gakken Mook神社紀行セレクションvol.1、[[薗田稔]]監修、学研マーケティング、2013年7月4日、82p. ISBN 978-4-05-610047-1 * 金子延夫『玉城町史 第1巻―南伊勢の歴史と伝承―』三重県郷土資料叢書第61集、三重県郷土史料刊行会、昭和58年8月10日、276p. * 式内社研究会 編『式内社調査報告 第六巻 東海道1』[[皇學館大学]]出版部、平成2年2月28日、690p. ISBN 4-87644-080-8 * [[福山敏男]]・[[稲垣栄三|稲垣榮三]]・村瀬美樹・胡麻鶴醇之『神宮―第六十回神宮式年遷宮―』[[小学館]]、昭和50年4月20日、246p. * 『日本人なら知っておきたい 伊勢神宮と125の社』タツミムック、[[辰巳出版]]、2013年11月1日、127p. ISBN 978-4-7778-1197-7

{{125 Shrines of Ise}}
{{神道 横}} {{125 Shrines of Ise|そないしんしや}} [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Sonai Shrine-qq]] [[ja:園相神社]][[Category:園相神社]][[Category:伊勢市の神社]] [[Category:摂末社]] [[Category:refreshing imported pages]][[Category:refreshing imported pages]] [[Category:bump 1-26]] [[Category:category refresh]] [[Category:pgoodbye $1]][[Category:new pages]][[Category:園相神社]][[Category:pgoodbye $1]][[Category:new pages]] [[Category:125 Shrines of Ise]] [[Category:園相神社]][[Category:125 Shrines of Ise]] [[Category:Sqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqq]] [[Category:qqqq]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:new pages]][[sn:Sonai Shrine]][[Category:new pages]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Sonai Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Sonai Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Sonai Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Sonai Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Sonai Shrine]][[Category:qq]]

revision.text:
{{Shrine | Name = {{ill|Sonai Shrine|ja|園相神社}} | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = [[File:Sonai-jinja 01.jpg|280px]] | location = 723 Shiraki, Tsumura-cho, [[Ise]], [[Mie Prefecture]], Japan | coordinates = {{coord|34|27|25.1|N|136|39|19.7|E|region:JP|display=inline}} | Map = Japan Mie | deity = * {{ill|Sonaibihiko-no-mikoto|ja|曽奈比比古命}} * {{ill|Mimae-no-kami|ja|御前神}} | type = [[Shikinaisha]] (minor rank), {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮}} auxiliary shrine (*sessha*) | Established = Unknown | Main Hall = [[Shinmei-zukuri]] | Other Names = Shiraki-san (白木さん) | festival = [[Kannamesai]] (Harvest Festival) | Rituals = }}
'''{{ill|Sonai Shrine|ja|園相神社}}''' (*Sonai Jinja*) is an auxiliary shrine (*sessha*) of {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮}} (the Inner Shrine of the [[Ise Grand Shrine]]), located in [[Ise]], [[Mie Prefecture]], Japan. It ranks third among the auxiliary shrines of the Inner Shrine.<ref>Ujiyamada City Office (1929): pp. 9–11.</ref>
The shrine enshrines deities who serve as the guardians of the sacred gardens (*御園*, *misono*) of the Grand Shrine.<ref name="gk">Gakken Publishing (2013): p. 65.</ref><ref name="ic">Ise Bunka Publishing (2008): p. 71.</ref>
== Overview ==
Located at 723 Shiraki, Tsumura-chō, {{ill|Ise, Mie|ja|伊勢市}}, {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県}}.<ref name="tm"/> This shrine is said to have been established by {{ill|Yamatohime-no-mikoto|ja|倭姫命|1=Yamatohime-no-mikoto}}.<ref name="gk"/> Locally, it is affectionately referred to as "Shiraki-san" or "Shiroki-no-miya," and has garnered deep devotion.<ref name="ic"/>
Walking through a straight path lined with tall {{ill|cedar trees|ja|杉}}, one reaches the shrine buildings.<ref name="ic"/> At the entrance of the path stands a stone inscribed in {{ill|Kyōhō|ja|享保}} (1724), prohibiting the killing of living beings.<ref name="sk1">Shikinaisha Research Association (1990): p. 221</ref> As of 1990 (Heisei 2), the shrine grounds covered 5,609 m², but 753.9 m² were transferred to Mie Prefecture for the expansion of {{ill|Mie Prefectural Route 22 Ise-Nantō Line|ja|三重県道22号伊勢南島線}}.<ref name="sk1"/> The shrine buildings are constructed in the {{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|ja|神明造}} style with board roofing, measuring approximately 9 shaku (≈2.7 m) in length, 7 shaku (≈2.1 m) in width, and 7.8 shaku (≈2.4 m) in height.<ref name="sk1"/> They are equipped with a sacred fence ({{ill|tamagaki|ja|玉垣}}), a tamagaki gate, and a {{ill|torii|ja|鳥居}}.<ref name="sk1"/>
In ancient times, there were two shrine buildings, each measuring 9 shaku (≈2.7 m) in length, 7.5 shaku (≈2.3 m) in width, and 4.6 shaku (≈1.4 m) in height.<ref name="jg">Fukuyama et al. (1975): p. 150</ref> The sacred object is a stone.<ref name="jg"/>
== Enshrined Deities ==
The enshrined deities are Sonaibihiko-no-Mikoto (曽奈比比古命) and Gozen-no-Kami (御前神).<ref name="gk"/><ref name="tm"/><ref name="ic"/> They are local deities of this region<ref name="gk"/> and protectors of the sacred gardens of Ise Grand Shrine.<ref name="ic"/> Sonaibihiko-no-Mikoto is the child of Ōminakami-no-Mikoto (大水上命) and is known as the deity of garden creation.<ref name="tm"/>
There was once a belief that the shrine could cure toothaches, and it was customary to offer 12 pairs of chopsticks made from bush clover as a token of gratitude upon recovery.<ref name="ic"/>
In ancient times, there was an independent shrine dedicated to Gozen-no-Kami, but after its reconstruction in {{ill|Bunei|ja|文永}} 3 (1266), no further reconstructions took place. Eventually, the shrine was not rebuilt, and Gozen-no-Kami came to be enshrined together with Sonaibihiko-no-Mikoto in the main shrine.<ref>Shikinaisha Research Association (1990): pp. 219–220</ref>
== History ==
It is said that when {{ill|Yamatohime-no-mikoto|ja|倭姫命|1=Yamatohime-no-mikoto}} traveled along the {{ill|Miyagawa River (Mie)|ja|宮川}}, she dedicated sacred gardens to {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神}} at this location, thus establishing the shrine.<ref name="ic"/><ref name="sk0">Shikinaisha Research Association (1990): p. 220</ref> Shrines enshrining {{ill|kunitsukami|ja|国津神}} were traditionally rebuilt using public funds, but six highly ranked shrines, including this one, were rebuilt by the shrine’s construction officials after the completion of the Inner Shrine’s transfer ceremony.<ref>Kaneko (1983): pp. 60–61</ref> Records of such a ceremony can be found in the ''Bunei 3 Naikū Sengu-ki''.<ref name="sk9">Shikinaisha Research Association (1990): p. 219</ref>
However, from around the {{ill|Ōnin era|ja|応仁}} (1467–1468), reconstruction efforts began to delay, and eventually, not only did reconstruction cease, but the location of the shrine grounds also became unknown.<ref name="sk9"/>
In {{ill|Kanbun|ja|寛文}} 3 (1663), the chief priest {{ill|Kawabe Masanaga|ja|河邊精長}} (Ōnakatomi Masanaga) conducted a search for the old shrine grounds. Referring to descriptions in the ''Kōtai Jingū Gishiki-chō'' such as "located in Numaki-go Sekiryo," the shrine was reconstructed in Sekiryo Village (present-day {{ill|Tamaki, Mie|ja|玉城町}} Sekiryo) on {{ill|September 22|ja|9月22日}} (Gregorian calendar: October 22, 1663), and the deity was transferred on {{ill|December 9|ja|12月9日}} (Gregorian calendar: January 7, 1664).<ref name="sk9"/>
However, doubts arose regarding this location, and on {{ill|December 23|ja|12月23日}} of {{ill|Genroku|ja|元禄}} 4 (Gregorian calendar: February 9, 1692), Ōnakatomi Nagaharu determined that Tsumura was the original site. The shrine was relocated to its current location on {{ill|March 26|ja|3月26日}} of Genroku 7 (Gregorian calendar: April 20, 1694).<ref name="sk9"/>
The shrine was rebuilt in March {{ill|1958|ja|1958年}} (Shōwa 33), and major renovations were carried out in April {{ill|1981|ja|1981年}} (Shōwa 56).<ref name="sk1"/>
== Rituals ==
During major festivals such as {{ill|Tsukinami-sai|ja|月次祭}}, {{ill|Kannamesai|ja|神嘗祭}}, {{ill|Kinensai|ja|祈年祭}}, {{ill|Niinamesai|ja|新嘗祭}}, and temporary offerings, priests visit the shrine to conduct rituals at the shrine front.<ref name="sk0"/> Other ceremonies, such as the {{ill|Saitan-sai|ja|歳旦祭}}, are conducted remotely from the Gojōden of the Inner Shrine.<ref name="sk0"/>
Additionally, the role of {{ill|hafuri (Shinto)|ja|祝部}} is designated to carry out daily maintenance and shrine duties. The hafuri are appointed by the Jingū Administration Office.<ref name="sk0"/>
== Flora ==
[[File:Sonai-jinja 02.jpg|thumb|Sacred forest of Sonai Shrine and Mie Prefectural Route 22 Ise-Nantō Line]] The shrine forest is composed of large trees that survived the {{ill|Isewan Typhoon|ja|伊勢湾台風}}, including {{ill|Japanese cypress|ja|ヒノキ}}, {{ill|cedar|ja|スギ}}, and {{ill|Japanese black pine|ja|クロマツ}}. It also contains a mixture of evergreen broadleaf trees such as {{ill|sakaki|ja|サカキ}}, {{ill|Japanese chinquapin|ja|シイ}}, {{ill|black oak|ja|クロバイ}}, and {{ill|Japanese cleyera|ja|ヒサカキ}}, along with deciduous broadleaf trees.<ref name="sk0"/>
== Surroundings ==
The shrine entrance faces {{ill|Mie Prefectural Route 22 Ise-Nantō Line|ja|三重県道22号伊勢南島線}}.<ref name="gk"/><ref>Ise Bunka-sha (2008): p. 70</ref> Despite its location along the roadway, the precincts remain peaceful and serene.<ref name="ic"/> Visitors coming from {{ill|Yamada (Ise)|ja|山田 (伊勢市)}} pass under the overpass of the {{ill|Ise Expressway|ja|伊勢自動車道}} to reach the shrine.<ref name="ic"/>
* From {{ill|Ise-shi Station|ja|伊勢市駅}} on the {{ill|Sangū Line|ja|参宮線}} operated by {{ill|JR Central|ja|東海旅客鉄道}} or the {{ill|Kintetsu Yamada Line|ja|近鉄山田線}} operated by {{ill|Kintetsu|ja|近畿日本鉄道}}, take a {{ill|Mie Kotsu|ja|三重交通}} bus and get off at the ''Shiraki'' bus stop. The shrine is located immediately nearby.<ref name="tm"/> (Approx. 7.5 km bus distance,<ref name="sk9"/> about 19 minutes.<ref name="tm"/>)
* By car, it takes about 11 minutes (approx. 7.5 km) from {{ill|Tamaki IC|ja|玉城インターチェンジ}} on the Ise Expressway, or about 27 minutes (approx. 7.3 km) from {{ill|Ise-nishi IC|ja|伊勢西インターチェンジ}}.
