== External Links == {{ external media | align = | width = 230px | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image1 = [http://www.sengukan.jp/wp-content/themes/sengukan/images/shrine/area05/area05_01.jpg 園相神社] }} * [http://www.jingukaikan.jp/125mairi/m06/0608.html 園相神社（そないじんじゃ）] - 一般財団法人伊勢神宮崇敬会
{{神道 横}}
{{125 Shrines of Ise}}
(1467–1468), reconstruction efforts began to delay, and eventually, not only did reconstruction cease, but the location of the shrine grounds also became unknown.<ref name="sk9"/>
In {{ill|Kanbun|ja|寛文|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanbun|qq=}} 3 (1663), the chief priest {{ill|Kawabe Masanaga|ja|河邊精長|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kawabe Masanaga|qq=}} (Ōnakatomi Masanaga) conducted a search for the old shrine grounds. Referring to descriptions in the ''Kōtai Jingū Gishiki-chō'' such as "located in Numaki-go Sekiryo," the shrine was reconstructed in Sekiryo Village (present-day {{ill|Tamaki, Mie|ja|玉城町|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamaki, Mie|qq=}} Sekiryo) on {{ill|September 22|ja|9月22日|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/September 22|qq=}} (Gregorian calendar: October 22, 1663), and the deity was transferred on {{ill|December 9|ja|12月9日|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/December 9|qq=}} (Gregorian calendar: January 7, 1664).<ref name="sk9"/>
However, doubts arose regarding this location, and on {{ill|December 23|ja|12月23日|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/December 23|qq=}} of {{ill|Genroku|ja|元禄|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Genroku|qq=}} 4 (Gregorian calendar: February 9, 1692), Ōnakatomi Nagaharu determined that Tsumura was the original site. The shrine was relocated to its current location on {{ill|March 26|ja|3月26日|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/March 26|qq=}} of Genroku 7 (Gregorian calendar: April 20, 1694).<ref name="sk9"/>
The shrine was rebuilt in March {{ill|1958|ja|1958年|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1958|qq=}} (Shōwa 33), and major renovations were carried out in April {{ill|1981|ja|1981st year|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1981|qq=}} (Shōwa 56).<ref name="sk1"/>
== Rituals ==
During major festivals such as {{ill|Tsukinami-sai|ja|月次祭|1=Tsukinami-no-Matsuri|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukinami-sai|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukinami-no-Matsuri|qq=}}, {{ill|Kannamesai Festival|lt=Kannamesai Festival|lt=Kannamesai|ja|神嘗祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kannamesai|qq=}}, {{ill|Kinensai|ja|祈年祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinensai|qq=}}, {{ill|Niiname-no-Matsuri|lt=Niiname-no-Matsuri|lt=Niinamesai|ja|新嘗祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Niinamesai|qq=}}, and temporary offerings, priests visit the shrine to conduct rituals at the shrine front.<ref name="sk0"/> Other ceremonies, such as the {{ill|Saitan-sai|ja|歳旦祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saitan-sai|qq=}}, are conducted remotely from the Gojōden of the Inner Shrine.<ref name="sk0"/>
Additionally, the role of {{ill|Hōri|lt=Hōri|lt=Hafuri (Shinto)|ja|祝部|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/hafuri (Shinto)|qq=}} is designated to carry out daily maintenance and shrine duties. The hafuri are appointed by the Jingū Administration Office.<ref name="sk0"/>
== Flora ==

revision.text:
{{Shrine | Name = {{ill|Sonai Shrine|ja|園相神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sonai Shrine|qq=}} | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = [[File:Sonai-jinja 01.jpg|280px]] | location = 723 Shiraki, Tsumura-cho, [[Ise]], [[Mie Prefecture]], Japan | coordinates = {{coord|34|27|25.1|N|136|39|19.7|E|region:JP|display=inline}} | Map = Japan Mie | deity = ** {{ill|Sonaibihiko-no-mikoto|ja|曽奈比比古命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sonaibihiko-no-mikoto|qq=}} ** {{ill|Mimae-no-kami|ja|御前神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mimae-no-kami|qq=}} | type = [[Shikinaisha]] (minor Rank), {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jingū|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jingū|qq=}} auxiliary shrine (*sessha*) | Established = Unknown | Main Hall = [[Shinmei-zukuri]] | Other Names = Shiraki-san (白木さん) | festival = [[Kannamesai Festival|Kannamesai]] (Harvest Festival) | Rituals = }}
'''{{ill|Sonai Shrine|ja|園相神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sonai Shrine|qq=}}''' (*Sonai Jinja*) is an auxiliary shrine (*sessha*) of {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jingū|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jingū|qq=}} (the Inner Shrine of the [[Ise Grand Shrine]]), located in [[Ise]], [[Mie Prefecture]], Japan. It Ranks third among the auxiliary shrines of the Inner Shrine.<ref>Ujiyamada City Office (1929): pp. 9–11.</ref>
The shrine enshrines deities who serve as the guardians of the sacred gardens (*御園*, *misono*) of the Grand Shrine.<ref name="gk">Gakken Publishing (2013): p. 65.</ref><ref name="ic">Ise Bunka Publishing (2008): p. 71.</ref>
== Overview ==
Located at 723 Shiraki, Tsumura-chō, {{ill|Ise, Mie|ja|伊勢市|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise, Mie|qq=}}, {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mie Prefecture|qq=}}.<ref name="tm"/> This shrine is said to have been established by {{ill|Yamatohime-no-mikoto|ja|倭姫命|1=Yamatohime-no-mikoto|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamatohime-no-mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamatohime-no-mikoto|qq=}}.<ref name="gk"/> Locally, it is affectionately referred to as "Shiraki-san" or "Shiroki-no-miya," and has garnered deep devotion.<ref name="ic"/>
Walking through a straight path lined with tall {{ill|cedar trees|ja|杉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/cedar trees|qq=}}, one reaches the shrine buildings.<ref name="ic"/> At the entrance of the path stands a stone inscribed in {{ill|Kyōhō|ja|享保|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyōhō|qq=}} (1724), prohibiting the killing of living beings.<ref name="sk1">Shikinaisha Research Association (1990): p. 221</ref> As of 1990 (Heisei 2), the shrine grounds covered 5,609 m², but 753.9 m² were transferred to Mie Prefecture for the expansion of {{ill|Mie Prefectural Route 22 Ise-Nantō Line|ja|三重県道22号伊勢南島線|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mie Prefectural Route 22 Ise-Nantō Line|qq=}}.<ref name="sk1"/> The shrine buildings are constructed in the {{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|ja|神明造|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinmei-zukuri|qq=}} style with board roofing, measuring approximately 9 shaku (≈2.7 m) in length, 7 shaku (≈2.1 m) in width, and 7.8 shaku (≈2.4 m) in height.<ref name="sk1"/> They are equipped with a sacred fence ({{ill|tamagaki|ja|玉垣|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/tamagaki|qq=}}), a tamagaki gate, and a {{ill|torii|ja|鳥居|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/torii|qq=}}.<ref name="sk1"/>
In ancient times, there were two shrine buildings, each measuring 9 shaku (≈2.7 m) in length, 7.5 shaku (≈2.3 m) in width, and 4.6 shaku (≈1.4 m) in height.<ref name="jg">Fukuyama et al. (1975): p. 150</ref> The sacred object is a stone.<ref name="jg"/>
== Enshrined Deities ==
The enshrined deities are Sonaibihiko-no-Mikoto (曽奈比比古命) and Gozen-no-Kami (御前神).<ref name="gk"/><ref name="tm"/><ref name="ic"/> They are local deities of this region<ref name="gk"/> and protectors of the sacred gardens of Ise Grand Shrine.<ref name="ic"/> Sonaibihiko-no-Mikoto is the child of Ōminakami-no-Mikoto (大水上命) and is known as the deity of garden creation.<ref name="tm"/>
There was once a belief that the shrine could cure toothaches, and it was customary to offer 12 pairs of chopsticks made from bush clover as a token of gratitude upon recovery.<ref name="ic"/>
In ancient times, there was an independent shrine dedicated to Gozen-no-Kami, but after its reconstruction in {{ill|Bunei|ja|文永|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bunei|qq=}} 3 (1266), no further reconstructions took place. Eventually, the shrine was not rebuilt, and Gozen-no-Kami came to be enshrined together with Sonaibihiko-no-Mikoto in the main shrine.<ref>Shikinaisha Research Association (1990): pp. 219–220</ref>
== History ==
It is said that when {{ill|Yamatohime-no-mikoto|ja|倭姫命|1=Yamatohime-no-mikoto|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamatohime-no-mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamatohime-no-mikoto|qq=}} traveled along the {{ill|Miyagawa River (Mie)|ja|宮川|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miyagawa River (Mie)|qq=}}, she dedicated sacred gardens to {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神|1=Amaterasu|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amaterasu|13=User:Immanuelle/Amaterasu|qq=}} at this location, thus establishing the shrine.<ref name="ic"/><ref name="sk0">Shikinaisha Research Association (1990): p. 220</ref> Shrines enshrining {{ill|kunitsukami|ja|国津神|1=Kunitsukami|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kunitsukami|13=User:Immanuelle/Kunitsukami|qq=}} were traditionally rebuilt using public funds, but six highly Ranked shrines, including this one, were rebuilt by the shrine’s construction officials after the Completion tier 4]] The shrine forest is composed of large trees that survived the {{ill|Isewan Typhoon|ja|伊勢湾台風|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Isewan Typhoon|qq=}}, including {{ill|Japanese cypress|ja|ヒノキ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese cypress|qq=}}, {{ill|cedar|ja|スギ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/cedar|qq=}}, and {{ill|Japanese black pine|ja|クロマツ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese black pine|qq=}}. It also contains a mixture of evergreen broadleaf trees such as {{ill|sakaki|ja|サカキ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/sakaki|qq=}}, {{ill|Japanese chinquapin|ja|シイ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese chinquapin|qq=}}, {{ill|black oak|ja|クロバイ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/black oak|qq=}}, and {{ill|Japanese cleyera|ja|ヒサカキ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese cleyera|qq=}}, along with deciduous broadleaf trees.<ref name="sk0"/>
== Surroundings ==
The shrine entrance faces {{ill|Mie Prefectural Route 22 Ise-Nantō Line|ja|三重県道22号伊勢南島線|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mie Prefectural Route 22 Ise-Nantō Line|qq=}}.<ref name="gk"/><ref>Ise Bunka-sha (2008): p. 70</ref> Despite its location along the roadway, the precincts remain peaceful and serene.<ref name="ic"/> Visitors coming from {{ill|Yamada (Ise)|ja|山田 (伊勢市)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamada (Ise)|qq=}} pass under the overpass of the {{ill|Ise Expressway|ja|伊勢自動車道|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Expressway|qq=}} to reach the shrine.<ref name="ic"/>
* From {{ill|Ise-shi Station|ja|伊勢市駅|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise-shi Station|qq=}} on the {{ill|Sangū Line|ja|参宮線|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sangū Line|qq=}} operated by {{ill|JR Central|ja|東海旅客鉄道|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/JR Central|qq=}} or the {{ill|Kintetsu Yamada Line|ja|近鉄山田線|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kintetsu Yamada Line|qq=}} operated by {{ill|Kintetsu|ja|近畿日本鉄道|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kintetsu|qq=}}, take a {{ill|Mie Kotsu|ja|三重交通|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mie Kotsu|qq=}} bus and get off at the ''Shiraki'' bus stop. The shrine is located immediately nearby.<ref name="tm"/> (Approx. 7.5 km bus distance,<ref name="sk9"/> about 19 minutes.<ref name="tm"/>)
* By car, it takes about 11 minutes (approx. 7.5 km) from {{ill|Tamaki IC|ja|玉城インターチェンジ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamaki IC|qq=}} on the Ise Expressway, or about 27 minutes (approx. 7.3 km) from {{ill|Ise-nishi IC|ja|伊勢西インターチェンジ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise-nishi IC|qq=}}.

The shrine was rebuilt in March {{ill|1958|ja|1958年|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1958|qq=}} (Shōwa 33), and major renovations were carried out in April {{ill|1981|ja|1981年|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1981|qq=}} (Shōwa 56).<ref name="sk1"/>
== Rituals ==
During major festivals such as {{ill|Tsukinami-sai|ja|月次祭|1=Tsukinami-no-Matsuri|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukinami-sai|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukinami-no-Matsuri|qq=}}, {{ill|Kannamesai Festival|lt=Kannamesai Festival|lt=Kannamesai|ja|神嘗祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kannamesai|qq=}}, {{ill|Kinensai|ja|祈年祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinensai|qq=}}, {{ill|Niiname-no-Matsuri|lt=Niiname-no-Matsuri|lt=Niinamesai|ja|新嘗祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Niinamesai|qq=}}, and temporary offerings, priests visit the shrine to conduct rituals at the shrine front.<ref name="sk0"/> Other ceremonies, such as the {{ill|Saitan-sai|ja|歳旦祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saitan-sai|qq=}}, are conducted remotely from the Gojōden of the Inner Shrine.<ref name="sk0"/>
Additionally, the role of {{ill|Hōri|lt=Hōri|lt=Hafuri (Shinto)|ja|祝部|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/hafuri (Shinto)|qq=}} is designated to carry out daily maintenance and shrine duties. The hafuri are appointed by the Jingū Administration Office.<ref name="sk0"/>
== Flora ==
[[File:Sonai-jinja 02.jpg|thumb|Sacred forest of Sonai Shrine and Mie Prefectural Route 22 Ise-Nantō Line]]
}}

revision.text:
{{Shrine | Name = {{ill|Sonai Shrine|ja|園相神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sonai Shrine|qq=}} | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = [[File:Sonai-jinja 01.jpg|280px]] | location = 723 Shiraki, Tsumura-cho, [[Ise]], [[Mie Prefecture]], Japan | coordinates = {{coord|34|27|25.1|N|136|39|19.7|E|region:JP|display=inline}} | Map = Japan Mie | deity = ** {{ill|Sonaibihiko-no-mikoto|ja|曽奈比比古命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sonaibihiko-no-mikoto|qq=}} ** {{ill|Mimae-no-kami|ja|御前神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mimae-no-kami|qq=}} | type = [[Shikinaisha]] (minor Rank), {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jingū|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jingū|qq=}} auxiliary shrine (*sessha*) | Established = Unknown | Main Hall = [[Shinmei-zukuri]] | Other Names = Shiraki-san (白木さん) | festival = [[Kannamesai Festival|Kannamesai]] (Harvest Festival) | Rituals = }}
'''{{ill|Sonai Shrine|ja|園相神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sonai Shrine|qq=}}''' (*Sonai Jinja*) is an auxiliary shrine (*sessha*) of {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jingū|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jingū|qq=}} (the Inner Shrine of the [[Ise Grand Shrine]]), located in [[Ise]], [[Mie Prefecture]], Japan. It Ranks third among the auxiliary shrines of the Inner Shrine.<ref>Ujiyamada City Office (1929): pp. 9–11.</ref>
The shrine enshrines deities who serve as the guardians of the sacred gardens (*御園*, *misono*) of the Grand Shrine.<ref name="gk">Gakken Publishing (2013): p. 65.</ref><ref name="ic">Ise Bunka Publishing (2008): p. 71.</ref>
== Overview ==
Located at 723 Shiraki, Tsumura-chō, [[:en:Ise, Mie|Ise, Mie]], [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]].<ref name="tm"/> This shrine is said to have been established by [[:en:Yamatohime-no-mikoto|Yamatohime-no-mikoto]].<ref name="gk"/> Locally, it is affectionately referred to as "Shiraki-san" or "Shiroki-no-miya," and has garnered deep devotion.<ref name="ic"/>
Walking through a straight path lined with tall {{ill|cedar trees|ja|杉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/cedar trees|qq=}}, one reaches the shrine buildings.<ref name="ic"/> At the entrance of the path stands a stone inscribed in [[:en:Kyōhō|Kyōhō]] (1724), prohibiting the killing of living beings.<ref name="sk1">Shikinaisha Research Association (1990): p. 221</ref> As of 1990 (Heisei 2), the shrine grounds covered 5,609 m², but 753.9 m² were transferred to Mie Prefecture for the expansion of {{ill|Mie Prefectural Route 22 Ise-Nantō Line|ja|三重県道22号伊勢南島線|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mie Prefectural Route 22 Ise-Nantō Line|qq=}}.<ref name="sk1"/> The shrine buildings are constructed in the [[:en:Shinmei-zukuri|Shinmei-zukuri]] style with board roofing, measuring approximately 9 shaku (≈2.7 m) in length, 7 shaku (≈2.1 m) in width, and 7.8 shaku (≈2.4 m) in height.<ref name="sk1"/> They are equipped with a sacred fence ([[:en:Tamagaki|tamagaki]]), a tamagaki gate, and a [[:en:Torii|torii]].<ref name="sk1"/>
In ancient times, there were two shrine buildings, each measuring 9 shaku (≈2.7 m) in length, 7.5 shaku (≈2.3 m) in width, and 4.6 shaku (≈1.4 m) in height.<ref name="jg">Fukuyama et al. (1975): p. 150</ref> The sacred object is a stone.<ref name="jg"/>
== Enshrined Deities ==
The enshrined deities are Sonaibihiko-no-Mikoto (曽奈比比古命) and Gozen-no-Kami (御前神).<ref name="gk"/><ref name="tm"/><ref name="ic"/> They are local deities of this region<ref name="gk"/> and protectors of the sacred gardens of Ise Grand Shrine.<ref name="ic"/> Sonaibihiko-no-Mikoto is the child of Ōminakami-no-Mikoto (大水上命) and is known as the deity of garden creation.<ref name="tm"/>
There was once a belief that the shrine could cure toothaches, and it was customary to offer 12 pairs of chopsticks made from bush clover as a token of gratitude upon recovery.<ref name="ic"/>
In ancient times, there was an independent shrine dedicated to Gozen-no-Kami, but after its reconstruction in [[:en:Bun'ei|Bunei]] 3 (1266), no further reconstructions took place. Eventually, the shrine was not rebuilt, and Gozen-no-Kami came to be enshrined together with Sonaibihiko-no-Mikoto in the main shrine.<ref>Shikinaisha Research Association (1990): pp. 219–220</ref>
== History ==
It is said that when [[:en:Yamatohime-no-mikoto|Yamatohime-no-mikoto]] traveled along the [[:en:Miyagawa|Miyagawa River (Mie)]], she dedicated sacred gardens to [[:en:Amaterasu|Amaterasu Ōmikami]] at this location, thus establishing the shrine.<ref name="ic"/><ref name="sk0">Shikinaisha Research Association (1990): p. 220</ref> Shrines enshrining {{ill|kunitsukami|ja|国津神|1=Kunitsukami|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kunitsukami|13=User:Immanuelle/Kunitsukami|qq=}} were traditionally rebuilt using public funds, but six highly Ranked shrines, including this one, were rebuilt by the shrine’s construction officials after the Completion tier 4]] The shrine forest is composed of large trees that survived the [[:en:Typhoon Vera|Isewan Typhoon]], including [[:en:Chamaecyparis obtusa|Japanese cypress]], [[:en:Cryptomeria|cedar]], and [[:en:Pinus thunbergii|Japanese black pine]]. It also contains a mixture of evergreen broadleaf trees such as [[:en:Cleyera japonica|sakaki]], [[:en:Castanopsis|Japanese chinquapin]], {{ill|black oak|ja|クロバイ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/black oak|qq=}}, and [[:en:Eurya japonica|Japanese cleyera]], along with deciduous broadleaf trees.<ref name="sk0"/>
== Surroundings ==
The shrine entrance faces {{ill|Mie Prefectural Route 22 Ise-Nantō Line|ja|三重県道22号伊勢南島線|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mie Prefectural Route 22 Ise-Nantō Line|qq=}}.<ref name="gk"/><ref>Ise Bunka-sha (2008): p. 70</ref> Despite its location along the roadway, the precincts remain peaceful and serene.<ref name="ic"/> Visitors coming from {{ill|Yamada (Ise)|ja|山田 (伊勢市)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamada (Ise)|qq=}} pass under the overpass of the [[:en:Ise Expressway|Ise Expressway]] to reach the shrine.<ref name="ic"/>
* From [[:en:Iseshi Station|Ise-shi Station]] on the [[:en:Sangū Line|Sangū Line]] operated by [[:en:Central Japan Railway Company|JR Central]] or the [[:en:Yamada Line (Kintetsu)|Kintetsu Yamada Line]] operated by [[:en:Kintetsu Railway|Kintetsu]], take a [[:en:Mie Kotsu|Mie Kotsu]] bus and get off at the ''Shiraki'' bus stop. The shrine is located immediately nearby.<ref name="tm"/> (Approx. 7.5 km bus distance,<ref name="sk9"/> about 19 minutes.<ref name="tm"/>)
* By car, it takes about 11 minutes (approx. 7.5 km) from {{ill|Tamaki IC|ja|玉城インターチェンジ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamaki IC|qq=}} on the Ise Expressway, or about 27 minutes (approx. 7.3 km) from {{ill|Ise-nishi IC|ja|伊勢西インターチェンジ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise-nishi IC|qq=}}.
== References ==
{{Reflist|2}}

ISBN 978-4-900759-37-4 * 宇治山田市役所 編『宇治山田市史 上巻』宇治山田市役所、昭和4th year 1月20日、862p. * [[学研パブリッシング]]『伊勢神宮に行こう』Gakken Mook神社紀行セレクションvol.1、[[薗田稔]]監修、学研マーケティング、2013rd year 7月4日、82p. ISBN 978-4-05-610047-1 * 金子延夫『玉城町史 第1巻―南伊勢の歴史と伝承―』三重県郷土資料叢書第61集、三重県郷土史料刊行会、昭和58th year 8月10日、276p. * 式内社研究会 編『式内社調査報告 第六巻 東海道1』[[Kogakkan University]]出版部、平成2nd year 2月28日、690p. ISBN 4-87644-080-8 * [[福山敏男]]・[[稲垣栄三|稲垣榮三]]・村瀬美樹・胡麻鶴醇之『神宮―第六十回神宮式年遷宮―』[[小学館]]、昭和50th year 4月20日、246p. * 『日本人なら知っておきたい 伊勢神宮と125の社』タツミムック、[[辰巳出版]]、2013rd year 11月1日、127p. ISBN 978-4-7778-1197-7 == See Also == * {{ill|List of 125 Shrines of Ise|ja|神宮125社の一覧|lt=List of 125 Shrines of Ise|1=List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|3=伊勢国の式内社一覧|1=List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of 125 Shrines of Ise|13=User:Immanuelle/List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|qq=}} * [[宮本村 (三重県)]]
== External Links == {{ external media | align = | width = 230px | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image1 = [http://www.sengukan.jp/wp-content/themes/sengukan/images/shrine/area05/area05_01.jpg 園相神社] }} * [http://www.jingukaikan.jp/125mairi/m06/0608.html 園相神社（そないじんじゃ）] - 一般財団法人伊勢神宮崇敬会
{{神道 横}}
{{125 Shrines of Ise}}
(1467–1468), reconstruction efforts began to delay, and eventually, not only did reconstruction cease, but the location of the shrine grounds also became unknown.<ref name="sk9"/>
In [[:en:Kanbun (era)|Kanbun]] 3 (1663), the chief priest {{ill|Kawabe Masanaga|ja|河邊精長|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kawabe Masanaga|qq=}} (Ōnakatomi Masanaga) conducted a search for the old shrine grounds. Referring to descriptions in the ''Kōtai Jingū Gishiki-chō'' such as "located in Numaki-go Sekiryo," the shrine was reconstructed in Sekiryo Village (present-day [[:en:Tamaki, Mie|Tamaki, Mie]] Sekiryo) on [[:en:September 22|September 22]] (Gregorian calendar: October 22, 1663), and the deity was transferred on [[:en:December 9|December 9]] (Gregorian calendar: January 7, 1664).<ref name="sk9"/>
However, doubts arose regarding this location, and on [[:en:December 23|December 23]] of [[:en:Genroku|Genroku]] 4 (Gregorian calendar: February 9, 1692), Ōnakatomi Nagaharu determined that Tsumura was the original site. The shrine was relocated to its current location on [[:en:March 26|March 26]] of Genroku 7 (Gregorian calendar: April 20, 1694).<ref name="sk9"/>
The shrine was rebuilt in March [[:en:1958|1958]] (Shōwa 33), and major renovations were carried out in April [[:en:1981|1981]] (Shōwa 56).<ref name="sk1"/>
== Rituals ==
During major festivals such as {{ill|Tsukinami-sai|ja|月次祭|1=Tsukinami-no-Matsuri|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukinami-sai|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukinami-no-Matsuri|qq=}}, [[:en:Kannamesai Festival|Kannamesai]], [[:en:Kinen-sai|Kinensai]], [[:en:Niiname-no-Matsuri|Niinamesai]], and temporary offerings, priests visit the shrine to conduct rituals at the shrine front.<ref name="sk0"/> Other ceremonies, such as the {{ill|Saitan-sai|ja|歳旦祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saitan-sai|qq=}}, are conducted remotely from the Gojōden of the Inner Shrine.<ref name="sk0"/>
Additionally, the role of {{ill|Hōri|lt=Hōri|lt=Hafuri (Shinto)|ja|祝部|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/hafuri (Shinto)|qq=}} is designated to carry out daily maintenance and shrine duties. The hafuri are appointed by the Jingū Administration Office.<ref name="sk0"/>
== Flora ==

The shrine enshrines deities who serve as the guardians of the sacred gardens (*御園*, *misono*) of the Grand Shrine.<ref name="gk">Gakken Publishing (2013): p. 65.</ref><ref name="ic">Ise Bunka Publishing (2008): p. 71.</ref>

In ancient times, there were two shrine buildings, each measuring 9 shaku (≈2.7 m) in length, 7.5 shaku (≈2.3 m) in width, and 4.6 shaku (≈1.4 m) in height.<ref name="jg">Fukuyama et al. (1975): p. 150</ref> The sacred object is a stone.<ref name="jg"/>

The enshrined deities are Sonaibihiko-no-Mikoto (曽奈比比古命) and Gozen-no-Kami (御前神).<ref name="gk"/><ref name="tm"/><ref name="ic"/> They are local deities of this region<ref name="gk"/> and protectors of the sacred gardens of Ise Grand Shrine.<ref name="ic"/> Sonaibihiko-no-Mikoto is the child of Ōminakami-no-Mikoto (大水上命) and is known as the deity of garden creation.<ref name="tm"/>

In ancient times, there was an independent shrine dedicated to Gozen-no-Kami, but after its reconstruction in [[:en:Bun'ei|Bunei]] 3 (1266), no further reconstructions took place. Eventually, the shrine was not rebuilt, and Gozen-no-Kami came to be enshrined together with Sonaibihiko-no-Mikoto in the main shrine.<ref>Shikinaisha Research Association (1990): pp. 219–220</ref>

It also contains a mixture of evergreen broadleaf trees such as [[:en:Cleyera japonica|sakaki]], [[:en:Castanopsis|Japanese chinquapin]], {{ill|black oak|ja|クロバイ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/black oak|qq=}}, and [[:en:Eurya japonica|Japanese cleyera]], along with deciduous broadleaf trees.<ref name="sk0"/>

ISBN 4-87644-080-8 * [[福山敏男]]・[[稲垣栄三|稲垣榮三]]・村瀬美樹・胡麻鶴醇之『神宮―第六十回神宮式年遷宮―』[[小学館]]、昭和50th year 4月20日、246p. * 『日本人なら知っておきたい 伊勢神宮と125の社』タツミムック、[[辰巳出版]]、2013rd year 11月1日、127p. ISBN 978-4-7778-1197-7 == See Also == * {{ill|List of 125 Shrines of Ise|ja|神宮125社の一覧|lt=List of 125 Shrines of Ise|1=List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|3=伊勢国の式内社一覧|1=List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of 125 Shrines of Ise|13=User:Immanuelle/List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|qq=}} * [[宮本村 (三重県)]]
== External Links == {{ external media | align = | width = 230px | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image1 = [http://www.sengukan.jp/wp-content/themes/sengukan/images/shrine/area05/area05_01.jpg 園相神社] }} * [http://www.jingukaikan.jp/125mairi/m06/0608.html 園相神社（そないじんじゃ）] - 一般財団法人伊勢神宮崇敬会
{{神道 横}}
{{125 Shrines of Ise}}
(1467–1468), reconstruction efforts began to delay, and eventually, not only did reconstruction cease, but the location of the shrine grounds also became unknown.<ref name="sk9"/>
In [[:en:Kanbun (era)|Kanbun]] 3 (1663), the chief priest {{ill|Kawabe Masanaga|ja|河邊精長|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kawabe Masanaga|qq=}} (Ōnakatomi Masanaga) conducted a search for the old shrine grounds. Referring to descriptions in the ''Kōtai Jingū Gishiki-chō'' such as "located in Numaki-go Sekiryo," the shrine was reconstructed in Sekiryo Village (present-day [[:en:Tamaki, Mie|Tamaki, Mie]] Sekiryo) on [[:en:September 22|September 22]] (Gregorian calendar: October 22, 1663), and the deity was transferred on [[:en:December 9|December 9]] (Gregorian calendar: January 7, 1664).<ref name="sk9"/>
However, doubts arose regarding this location, and on [[:en:December 23|December 23]] of [[:en:Genroku|Genroku]] 4 (Gregorian calendar: February 9, 1692), Ōnakatomi Nagaharu determined that Tsumura was the original site. The shrine was relocated to its current location on [[:en:March 26|March 26]] of Genroku 7 (Gregorian calendar: April 20, 1694).<ref name="sk9"/>
The shrine was rebuilt in March [[:en:1958|1958]] (Shōwa 33), and major renovations were carried out in April [[:en:1981|1981]] (Shōwa 56).<ref name="sk1"/>
== Rituals ==
During major festivals such as {{ill|Tsukinami-sai|ja|月次祭|1=Tsukinami-no-Matsuri|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukinami-sai|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukinami-no-Matsuri|qq=}}, [[:en:Kannamesai Festival|Kannamesai]], [[:en:Kinen-sai|Kinensai]], [[:en:Niiname-no-Matsuri|Niinamesai]], and temporary offerings, priests visit the shrine to conduct rituals at the shrine front.<ref name="sk0"/> Other ceremonies, such as the {{ill|Saitan-sai|ja|歳旦祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saitan-sai|qq=}}, are conducted remotely from the Gojōden of the Inner Shrine.<ref name="sk0"/>
Additionally, the role of {{ill|Hōri|lt=Hōri|lt=Hafuri (Shinto)|ja|祝部|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/hafuri (Shinto)|qq=}} is designated to carry out daily maintenance and shrine duties. The hafuri are appointed by the Jingū Administration Office.<ref name="sk0"/>
== Flora ==
[[File:Sonai-jinja 02.jpg|thumb|Sacred forest of Sonai Shrine and Mie Prefectural Route 22 Ise-Nantō Line]]

revision.comment: Bot: add category Category:Setsumatsusha
revision.text:
{{Shrine | Name = {{ill|Sonai Shrine|ja|園相神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sonai Shrine|qq=}} | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = [[File:Sonai-jinja 01.jpg|280px]] | location = 723 Shiraki, Tsumura-cho, [[Ise]], [[Mie Prefecture]], Japan | coordinates = {{coord|34|27|25.1|N|136|39|19.7|E|region:JP|display=inline}} | Map = Japan Mie | deity = ** {{ill|Sonaibihiko-no-mikoto|ja|曽奈比比古命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sonaibihiko-no-mikoto|qq=}} ** {{ill|Mimae-no-kami|ja|御前神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mimae-no-kami|qq=}} | type = [[Shikinaisha]] (minor Rank), {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jingū|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jingū|qq=}} auxiliary shrine (*sessha*) | Established = Unknown | Main Hall = [[Shinmei-zukuri]] | Other Names = Shiraki-san (白木さん) | festival = [[Kannamesai Festival|Kannamesai]] (Harvest Festival) | Rituals = }}
'''{{ill|Sonai Shrine|ja|園相神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sonai Shrine|qq=}}''' (*Sonai Jinja*) is an auxiliary shrine (*sessha*) of {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jingū|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jingū|qq=}} (the Inner Shrine of the [[Ise Grand Shrine]]), located in [[Ise]], [[Mie Prefecture]], Japan. It Ranks third among the auxiliary shrines of the Inner Shrine.<ref>Ujiyamada City Office (1929): pp. 9–11.</ref>
The shrine enshrines deities who serve as the guardians of the sacred gardens (*御園*, *misono*) of the Grand Shrine.<ref name="gk">Gakken Publishing (2013): p. 65.</ref><ref name="ic">Ise Bunka Publishing (2008): p. 71.</ref>
== Overview ==
Located at 723 Shiraki, Tsumura-chō, [[:en:Ise, Mie|Ise, Mie]], [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]].<ref name="tm"/> This shrine is said to have been established by [[:en:Yamatohime-no-mikoto|Yamatohime-no-mikoto]].<ref name="gk"/> Locally, it is affectionately referred to as "Shiraki-san" or "Shiroki-no-miya," and has garnered deep devotion.<ref name="ic"/>
Walking through a straight path lined with tall {{ill|cedar trees|ja|杉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/cedar trees|qq=}}, one reaches the shrine buildings.<ref name="ic"/> At the entrance of the path stands a stone inscribed in [[:en:Kyōhō|Kyōhō]] (1724), prohibiting the killing of living beings.<ref name="sk1">Shikinaisha Research Association (1990): p. 221</ref> As of 1990 (Heisei 2), the shrine grounds covered 5,609 m², but 753.9 m² were transferred to Mie Prefecture for the expansion of {{ill|Mie Prefectural Route 22 Ise-Nantō Line|ja|三重県道22号伊勢南島線|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mie Prefectural Route 22 Ise-Nantō Line|qq=}}.<ref name="sk1"/> The shrine buildings are constructed in the [[:en:Shinmei-zukuri|Shinmei-zukuri]] style with board roofing, measuring approximately 9 shaku (≈2.7 m) in length, 7 shaku (≈2.1 m) in width, and 7.8 shaku (≈2.4 m) in height.<ref name="sk1"/> They are equipped with a sacred fence ([[:en:Tamagaki|tamagaki]]), a tamagaki gate, and a [[:en:Torii|torii]].<ref name="sk1"/>
In ancient times, there were two shrine buildings, each measuring 9 shaku (≈2.7 m) in length, 7.5 shaku (≈2.3 m) in width, and 4.6 shaku (≈1.4 m) in height.<ref name="jg">Fukuyama et al. (1975): p. 150</ref> The sacred object is a stone.<ref name="jg"/>
== Enshrined Deities ==
The enshrined deities are Sonaibihiko-no-Mikoto (曽奈比比古命) and Gozen-no-Kami (御前神).<ref name="gk"/><ref name="tm"/><ref name="ic"/> They are local deities of this region<ref name="gk"/> and protectors of the sacred gardens of Ise Grand Shrine.<ref name="ic"/> Sonaibihiko-no-Mikoto is the child of Ōminakami-no-Mikoto (大水上命) and is known as the deity of garden creation.<ref name="tm"/>
There was once a belief that the shrine could cure toothaches, and it was customary to offer 12 pairs of chopsticks made from bush clover as a token of gratitude upon recovery.<ref name="ic"/>
In ancient times, there was an independent shrine dedicated to Gozen-no-Kami, but after its reconstruction in [[:en:Bun'ei|Bunei]] 3 (1266), no further reconstructions took place. Eventually, the shrine was not rebuilt, and Gozen-no-Kami came to be enshrined together with Sonaibihiko-no-Mikoto in the main shrine.<ref>Shikinaisha Research Association (1990): pp. 219–220</ref>
== History ==
It is said that when [[:en:Yamatohime-no-mikoto|Yamatohime-no-mikoto]] traveled along the [[:en:Miyagawa|Miyagawa River (Mie)]], she dedicated sacred gardens to [[:en:Amaterasu|Amaterasu Ōmikami]] at this location, thus establishing the shrine.<ref name="ic"/><ref name="sk0">Shikinaisha Research Association (1990): p. 220</ref> Shrines enshrining {{ill|kunitsukami|ja|国津神|1=Kunitsukami|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kunitsukami|13=User:Immanuelle/Kunitsukami|qq=}} were traditionally rebuilt using public funds, but six highly Ranked shrines, including this one, were rebuilt by the shrine’s construction officials after the Completion tier 4]] The shrine forest is composed of large trees that survived the [[:en:Typhoon Vera|Isewan Typhoon]], including [[:en:Chamaecyparis obtusa|Japanese cypress]], [[:en:Cryptomeria|cedar]], and [[:en:Pinus thunbergii|Japanese black pine]]. It also contains a mixture of evergreen broadleaf trees such as [[:en:Cleyera japonica|sakaki]], [[:en:Castanopsis|Japanese chinquapin]], {{ill|black oak|ja|クロバイ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/black oak|qq=}}, and [[:en:Eurya japonica|Japanese cleyera]], along with deciduous broadleaf trees.<ref name="sk0"/>
== Surroundings ==
The shrine entrance faces {{ill|Mie Prefectural Route 22 Ise-Nantō Line|ja|三重県道22号伊勢南島線|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mie Prefectural Route 22 Ise-Nantō Line|qq=}}.<ref name="gk"/><ref>Ise Bunka-sha (2008): p. 70</ref> Despite its location along the roadway, the precincts remain peaceful and serene.<ref name="ic"/> Visitors coming from {{ill|Yamada (Ise)|ja|山田 (伊勢市)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamada (Ise)|qq=}} pass under the overpass of the [[:en:Ise Expressway|Ise Expressway]] to reach the shrine.<ref name="ic"/>
* From [[:en:Iseshi Station|Ise-shi Station]] on the [[:en:Sangū Line|Sangū Line]] operated by [[:en:Central Japan Railway Company|JR Central]] or the [[:en:Yamada Line (Kintetsu)|Kintetsu Yamada Line]] operated by [[:en:Kintetsu Railway|Kintetsu]], take a [[:en:Mie Kotsu|Mie Kotsu]] bus and get off at the ''Shiraki'' bus stop. The shrine is located immediately nearby.<ref name="tm"/> (Approx. 7.5 km bus distance,<ref name="sk9"/> about 19 minutes.<ref name="tm"/>)
* By car, it takes about 11 minutes (approx. 7.5 km) from {{ill|Tamaki IC|ja|玉城インターチェンジ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamaki IC|qq=}} on the Ise Expressway, or about 27 minutes (approx. 7.3 km) from {{ill|Ise-nishi IC|ja|伊勢西インターチェンジ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise-nishi IC|qq=}}.
== References ==

In [[:en:Kanbun (era)|Kanbun]] 3 (1663), the chief priest {{ill|Kawabe Masanaga|ja|河邊精長|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kawabe Masanaga|qq=}} (Ōnakatomi Masanaga) conducted a search for the old shrine grounds. The enshrined deities are Sonaibihiko-no-Mikoto (曽奈比比古命) and Gozen-no-Kami (御前神).<ref name="gk"/><ref name="tm"/><ref name="ic"/> They are local deities of this region<ref name="gk"/> and protectors of the sacred gardens of Ise Grand Shrine.<ref name="ic"/> Sonaibihiko-no-Mikoto is the child of Ōminakami-no-Mikoto (大水上命) and is known as the deity of garden creation.<ref name="tm"/>

In ancient times, there was an independent shrine dedicated to Gozen-no-Kami, but after its reconstruction in [[:en:Bun'ei|Bunei]] 3 (1266), no further reconstructions took place. Eventually, the shrine was not rebuilt, and Gozen-no-Kami came to be enshrined together with Sonaibihiko-no-Mikoto in the main shrine.<ref>Shikinaisha Research Association (1990): pp. 219–220</ref>
== History ==
It is said that when [[:en:Yamatohime-no-mikoto|Yamatohime-no-mikoto]] traveled along the [[:en:Miyagawa|Miyagawa River (Mie)]], she dedicated sacred gardens to [[:en:Amaterasu|Amaterasu Ōmikami]] at this location, thus establishing the shrine.<ref name="ic"/><ref name="sk0">Shikinaisha Research Association (1990): p. 220</ref> Shrines enshrining {{ill|kunitsukami|ja|国津神|1=Kunitsukami|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kunitsukami|13=User:Immanuelle/Kunitsukami|qq=}} were traditionally rebuilt using public funds, but six highly Ranked shrines, including this one, were rebuilt by the shrine’s construction officials after the Completion tier 4]] The shrine forest is composed of large trees that survived the [[:en:Typhoon Vera|Isewan Typhoon]], including [[:en:Chamaecyparis obtusa|Japanese cypress]], [[:en:Cryptomeria|cedar]], and [[:en:Pinus thunbergii|Japanese black pine]]. It also contains a mixture of evergreen broadleaf trees such as [[:en:Cleyera japonica|sakaki]], [[:en:Castanopsis|Japanese chinquapin]], {{ill|black oak|ja|クロバイ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/black oak|qq=}}, and [[:en:Eurya japonica|Japanese cleyera]], along with deciduous broadleaf trees.<ref name="sk0"/>
== Surroundings ==
The shrine entrance faces {{ill|Mie Prefectural Route 22 Ise-Nantō Line|ja|三重県道22号伊勢南島線|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mie Prefectural Route 22 Ise-Nantō Line|qq=}}.<ref name="gk"/><ref>Ise Bunka-sha (2008): p. 70</ref> Despite its location along the roadway, the precincts remain peaceful and serene.<ref name="ic"/> Visitors coming from {{ill|Yamada (Ise)|ja|山田 (伊勢市)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamada (Ise)|qq=}} pass under the overpass of the [[:en:Ise Expressway|Ise Expressway]] to reach the shrine.<ref name="ic"/>
* From [[:en:Iseshi Station|Ise-shi Station]] on the [[:en:Sangū Line|Sangū Line]] operated by [[:en:Central Japan Railway Company|JR Central]] or the [[:en:Yamada Line (Kintetsu)|Kintetsu Yamada Line]] operated by [[:en:Kintetsu Railway|Kintetsu]], take a [[:en:Mie Kotsu|Mie Kotsu]] bus and get off at the ''Shiraki'' bus stop. The shrine is located immediately nearby.<ref name="tm"/> (Approx. 7.5 km bus distance,<ref name="sk9"/> about 19 minutes.<ref name="tm"/>)
* By car, it takes about 11 minutes (approx. 7.5 km) from {{ill|Tamaki IC|ja|玉城インターチェンジ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamaki IC|qq=}} on the Ise Expressway, or about 27 minutes (approx. 7.3 km) from {{ill|Ise-nishi IC|ja|伊勢西インターチェンジ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise-nishi IC|qq=}}.

ISBN 4-87644-080-8 * [[福山敏男]]・[[稲垣栄三|稲垣榮三]]・村瀬美樹・胡麻鶴醇之『神宮―第六十回神宮式年遷宮―』[[小学館]]、昭和50th year 4月20日、246p. * 『日本人なら知っておきたい 伊勢神宮と125の社』タツミムック、[[辰巳出版]]、2013rd year 11月1日、127p. ISBN 978-4-7778-1197-7 == See Also == * {{ill|List of 125 Shrines of Ise|ja|神宮125社の一覧|lt=List of 125 Shrines of Ise|1=List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|3=伊勢国の式内社一覧|1=List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of 125 Shrines of Ise|13=User:Immanuelle/List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|qq=}} * [[宮本村 (三重県)]]
== External Links == {{ external media | align = | width = 230px | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image1 = [http://www.sengukan.jp/wp-content/themes/sengukan/images/shrine/area05/area05_01.jpg 園相神社] }} * [http://www.jingukaikan.jp/125mairi/m06/0608.html 園相神社（そないじんじゃ）] - 一般財団法人伊勢神宮崇敬会
{{神道 横}}
{{125 Shrines of Ise}}
(1467–1468), reconstruction efforts began to delay, and eventually, not only did reconstruction cease, but the location of the shrine grounds also became unknown.<ref name="sk9"/>
In [[:en:Kanbun (era)|Kanbun]] 3 (1663), the chief priest {{ill|Kawabe Masanaga|ja|河邊精長|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kawabe Masanaga|qq=}} (Ōnakatomi Masanaga) conducted a search for the old shrine grounds. Referring to descriptions in the ''Kōtai Jingū Gishiki-chō'' such as "located in Numaki-go Sekiryo," the shrine was reconstructed in Sekiryo Village (present-day [[:en:Tamaki, Mie|Tamaki, Mie]] Sekiryo) on [[:en:September 22|September 22]] (Gregorian calendar: October 22, 1663), and the deity was transferred on [[:en:December 9|December 9]] (Gregorian calendar: January 7, 1664).<ref name="sk9"/>
However, doubts arose regarding this location, and on [[:en:December 23|December 23]] of [[:en:Genroku|Genroku]] 4 (Gregorian calendar: February 9, 1692), Ōnakatomi Nagaharu determined that Tsumura was the original site. The shrine was relocated to its current location on [[:en:March 26|March 26]] of Genroku 7 (Gregorian calendar: April 20, 1694).<ref name="sk9"/>
The shrine was rebuilt in March [[:en:1958|1958]] (Shōwa 33), and major renovations were carried out in April [[:en:1981|1981]] (Shōwa 56).<ref name="sk1"/>
== Rituals ==
During major festivals such as {{ill|Tsukinami-sai|ja|月次祭|1=Tsukinami-no-Matsuri|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukinami-sai|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukinami-no-Matsuri|qq=}}, [[:en:Kannamesai Festival|Kannamesai]], [[:en:Kinen-sai|Kinensai]], [[:en:Niiname-no-Matsuri|Niinamesai]], and temporary offerings, priests visit the shrine to conduct rituals at the shrine front.<ref name="sk0"/> Other ceremonies, such as the {{ill|Saitan-sai|ja|歳旦祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saitan-sai|qq=}}, are conducted remotely from the Gojōden of the Inner Shrine.<ref name="sk0"/>
Additionally, the role of {{ill|Hōri|lt=Hōri|lt=Hafuri (Shinto)|ja|祝部|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/hafuri (Shinto)|qq=}} is designated to carry out daily maintenance and shrine duties. The hafuri are appointed by the Jingū Administration Office.<ref name="sk0"/>
== Flora ==
[[File:Sonai-jinja 02.jpg|thumb|Sacred forest of Sonai Shrine and Mie Prefectural Route 22 Ise-Nantō Line]]

{{Shrine | Name = {{ill|Sonai Shrine|ja|園相神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sonai Shrine|qq=}} | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = [[File:Sonai-jinja 01.jpg|280px]] | location = 723 Shiraki, Tsumura-cho, [[Ise]], [[Mie Prefecture]], Japan | coordinates = {{coord|34|27|25.1|N|136|39|19.7|E|region:JP|display=inline}} | Map = Japan Mie | deity = ** {{ill|Sonaibihiko-no-mikoto|ja|曽奈比比古命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sonaibihiko-no-mikoto|qq=}} ** {{ill|Mimae-no-kami|ja|御前神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mimae-no-kami|qq=}} | type = [[Shikinaisha]] (minor Rank), {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jingū|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jingū|qq=}} auxiliary shrine (*sessha*) | Established = Unknown | Main Hall = [[Shinmei-zukuri]] | Other Names = Shiraki-san (白木さん) | festival = [[Kannamesai Festival|Kannamesai]] (Harvest Festival) | Rituals = }}
'''{{ill|Sonai Shrine|ja|園相神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sonai Shrine|qq=}}''' (*Sonai Jinja*) is an auxiliary shrine (*sessha*) of {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jingū|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jingū|qq=}} (the Inner Shrine of the [[Ise Grand Shrine]]), located in [[Ise]], [[Mie Prefecture]], Japan. It Ranks third among the auxiliary shrines of the Inner Shrine.<ref>Ujiyamada City Office (1929): pp. 9–11.</ref>
The shrine enshrines deities who serve as the guardians of the sacred gardens (*御園*, *misono*) of the Grand Shrine.<ref name="gk">Gakken Publishing (2013): p. 65.</ref><ref name="ic">Ise Bunka Publishing (2008): p. 71.</ref>
== Overview ==
Located at 723 Shiraki, Tsumura-chō, [[:en:Ise, Mie|Ise, Mie]], [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]].<ref name="tm"/> This shrine is said to have been established by [[:en:Yamatohime-no-mikoto|Yamatohime-no-mikoto]].<ref name="gk"/> Locally, it is affectionately referred to as "Shiraki-san" or "Shiroki-no-miya," and has garnered deep devotion.<ref name="ic"/>
Walking through a straight path lined with tall {{ill|cedar trees|ja|杉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/cedar trees|qq=}}, one reaches the shrine buildings.<ref name="ic"/> At the entrance of the path stands a stone inscribed in [[:en:Kyōhō|Kyōhō]] (1724), prohibiting the killing of living beings.<ref name="sk1">Shikinaisha Research Association (1990): p. 221</ref> As of 1990 (Heisei 2), the shrine grounds covered 5,609 m², but 753.9 m² were transferred to Mie Prefecture for the expansion of {{ill|Mie Prefectural Route 22 Ise-Nantō Line|ja|三重県道22号伊勢南島線|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mie Prefectural Route 22 Ise-Nantō Line|qq=}}.<ref name="sk1"/> The shrine buildings are constructed in the [[:en:Shinmei-zukuri|Shinmei-zukuri]] style with board roofing, measuring approximately 9 shaku (≈2.7 m) in length, 7 shaku (≈2.1 m) in width, and 7.8 shaku (≈2.4 m) in height.<ref name="sk1"/> They are equipped with a sacred fence ([[:en:Tamagaki|tamagaki]]), a tamagaki gate, and a [[:en:Torii|torii]].<ref name="sk1"/>
In ancient times, there were two shrine buildings, each measuring 9 shaku (≈2.7 m) in length, 7.5 shaku (≈2.3 m) in width, and 4.6 shaku (≈1.4 m) in height.<ref name="jg">Fukuyama et al. (1975): p. 150</ref> The sacred object is a stone.<ref name="jg"/>
== Enshrined Deities ==
The enshrined deities are Sonaibihiko-no-Mikoto (曽奈比比古命) and Gozen-no-Kami (御前神).<ref name="gk"/><ref name="tm"/><ref name="ic"/> They are local deities of this region<ref name="gk"/> and protectors of the sacred gardens of Ise Grand Shrine.<ref name="ic"/> Sonaibihiko-no-Mikoto is the child of Ōminakami-no-Mikoto (大水上命) and is known as the deity of garden creation.<ref name="tm"/>
There was once a belief that the shrine could cure toothaches, and it was customary to offer 12 pairs of chopsticks made from bush clover as a token of gratitude upon recovery.<ref name="ic"/>
In ancient times, there was an independent shrine dedicated to Gozen-no-Kami, but after its reconstruction in [[:en:Bun'ei|Bunei]] 3 (1266), no further reconstructions took place. Eventually, the shrine was not rebuilt, and Gozen-no-Kami came to be enshrined together with Sonaibihiko-no-Mikoto in the main shrine.<ref>Shikinaisha Research Association (1990): pp. 219–220</ref>
== History ==
It is said that when [[:en:Yamatohime-no-mikoto|Yamatohime-no-mikoto]] traveled along the [[:en:Miyagawa|Miyagawa River (Mie)]], she dedicated sacred gardens to [[:en:Amaterasu|Amaterasu Ōmikami]] at this location, thus establishing the shrine.<ref name="ic"/><ref name="sk0">Shikinaisha Research Association (1990): p. 220</ref> Shrines enshrining {{ill|kunitsukami|ja|国津神|1=Kunitsukami|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kunitsukami|13=User:Immanuelle/Kunitsukami|qq=}} were traditionally rebuilt using public funds, but six highly Ranked shrines, including this one, were rebuilt by the shrine’s construction officials after the Completion tier 4]] The shrine forest is composed of large trees that survived the [[:en:Typhoon Vera|Isewan Typhoon]], including [[:en:Chamaecyparis obtusa|Japanese cypress]], [[:en:Cryptomeria|cedar]], and [[:en:Pinus thunbergii|Japanese black pine]]. It also contains a mixture of evergreen broadleaf trees such as [[:en:Cleyera japonica|sakaki]], [[:en:Castanopsis|Japanese chinquapin]], {{ill|black oak|ja|クロバイ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/black oak|qq=}}, and [[:en:Eurya japonica|Japanese cleyera]], along with deciduous broadleaf trees.<ref name="sk0"/>
== Surroundings ==
The shrine entrance faces {{ill|Mie Prefectural Route 22 Ise-Nantō Line|ja|三重県道22号伊勢南島線|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mie Prefectural Route 22 Ise-Nantō Line|qq=}}.<ref name="gk"/><ref>Ise Bunka-sha (2008): p. 70</ref> Despite its location along the roadway, the precincts remain peaceful and serene.<ref name="ic"/> Visitors coming from {{ill|Yamada (Ise)|ja|山田 (伊勢市)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamada (Ise)|qq=}} pass under the overpass of the [[:en:Ise Expressway|Ise Expressway]] to reach the shrine.<ref name="ic"/>
* From [[:en:Iseshi Station|Ise-shi Station]] on the [[:en:Sangū Line|Sangū Line]] operated by [[:en:Central Japan Railway Company|JR Central]] or the [[:en:Yamada Line (Kintetsu)|Kintetsu Yamada Line]] operated by [[:en:Kintetsu Railway|Kintetsu]], take a [[:en:Mie Kotsu|Mie Kotsu]] bus and get off at the ''Shiraki'' bus stop. The shrine is located immediately nearby.<ref name="tm"/> (Approx. 7.5 km bus distance,<ref name="sk9"/> about 19 minutes.<ref name="tm"/>)
* By car, it takes about 11 minutes (approx. 7.5 km) from {{ill|Tamaki IC|ja|玉城インターチェンジ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamaki IC|qq=}} on the Ise Expressway, or about 27 minutes (approx. 7.3 km) from {{ill|Ise-nishi IC|ja|伊勢西インターチェンジ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise-nishi IC|qq=}}.

Referring to descriptions in the ''Kōtai Jingū Gishiki-chō'' such as "located in Numaki-go Sekiryo," the shrine was reconstructed in Sekiryo Village (present-day [[:en:Tamaki, Mie|Tamaki, Mie]] Sekiryo) on [[:en:September 22|September 22]] (Gregorian calendar: October 22, 1663), and the deity was transferred on [[:en:December 9|December 9]] (Gregorian calendar: January 7, 1664).<ref name="sk9"/>

'''{{ill|Sonai Shrine|ja|園相神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sonai Shrine|qq=}}''' (*Sonai Jinja*) is an auxiliary shrine (*sessha*) of {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jingū|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jingū|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} (the Inner Shrine of the [[Ise Grand Shrine]]), located in [[Ise]], [[Mie Prefecture]], Japan. It Ranks third among the auxiliary shrines of the Inner Shrine.<ref>Ujiyamada City Office (1929): pp. 9–11.</ref>
The shrine enshrines deities who serve as the guardians of the sacred gardens (*御園*, *misono*) of the Grand Shrine.<ref name="gk">Gakken Publishing (2013): p. 65.</ref><ref name="ic">Ise Bunka Publishing (2008): p. 71.</ref>
== Overview ==
Located at 723 Shiraki, Tsumura-chō, [[:en:Ise, Mie|Ise, Mie]], [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]].<ref name="tm"/> This shrine is said to have been established by [[:en:Yamatohime-no-mikoto|Yamatohime-no-mikoto]].<ref name="gk"/> Locally, it is affectionately referred to as "Shiraki-san" or "Shiroki-no-miya," and has garnered deep devotion.<ref name="ic"/>
Walking through a straight path lined with tall {{ill|cedar trees|ja|杉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/cedar trees|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スギ}}, one reaches the shrine buildings.<ref name="ic"/> At the entrance of the path stands a stone inscribed in [[:en:Kyōhō|Kyōhō]] (1724), prohibiting the killing of living beings.<ref name="sk1">Shikinaisha Research Association (1990): p. 221</ref> As of 1990 (Heisei 2), the shrine grounds covered 5,609 m², but 753.9 m² were transferred to Mie Prefecture for the expansion of {{ill|Mie Prefectural Route 22 Ise-Nantō Line|ja|三重県道22号伊勢南島線|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mie Prefectural Route 22 Ise-Nantō Line|qq=}}.<ref name="sk1"/> The shrine buildings are constructed in the [[:en:Shinmei-zukuri|Shinmei-zukuri]] style with board roofing, measuring approximately 9 shaku (≈2.7 m) in length, 7 shaku (≈2.1 m) in width, and 7.8 shaku (≈2.4 m) in height.<ref name="sk1"/> They are equipped with a sacred fence ([[:en:Tamagaki|tamagaki]]), a tamagaki gate, and a [[:en:Torii|torii]].<ref name="sk1"/>
In ancient times, there were two shrine buildings, each measuring 9 shaku (≈2.7 m) in length, 7.5 shaku (≈2.3 m) in width, and 4.6 shaku (≈1.4 m) in height.<ref name="jg">Fukuyama et al. (1975): p. 150</ref> The sacred object is a stone.<ref name="jg"/>
== Enshrined Deities ==
The enshrined deities are Sonaibihiko-no-Mikoto (曽奈比比古命) and Gozen-no-Kami (御前神).<ref name="gk"/><ref name="tm"/><ref name="ic"/> They are local deities of this region<ref name="gk"/> and protectors of the sacred gardens of Ise Grand Shrine.<ref name="ic"/> Sonaibihiko-no-Mikoto is the child of Ōminakami-no-Mikoto (大水上命) and is known as the deity of garden creation.<ref name="tm"/>
There was once a belief that the shrine could cure toothaches, and it was customary to offer 12 pairs of chopsticks made from bush clover as a token of gratitude upon recovery.<ref name="ic"/>
In ancient times, there was an independent shrine dedicated to Gozen-no-Kami, but after its reconstruction in [[:en:Bun'ei|Bunei]] 3 (1266), no further reconstructions took place. Eventually, the shrine was not rebuilt, and Gozen-no-Kami came to be enshrined together with Sonaibihiko-no-Mikoto in the main shrine.<ref>Shikinaisha Research Association (1990): pp. 219–220</ref>
== History ==
It is said that when [[:en:Yamatohime-no-mikoto|Yamatohime-no-mikoto]] traveled along the [[:en:Miyagawa|Miyagawa River (Mie)]], she dedicated sacred gardens to [[:en:Amaterasu|Amaterasu Ōmikami]] at this location, thus establishing the shrine.<ref name="ic"/><ref name="sk0">Shikinaisha Research Association (1990): p. 220</ref> Shrines enshrining {{ill|kunitsukami|ja|国津神|1=Kunitsukami|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kunitsukami|13=User:Immanuelle/Kunitsukami|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天津神・国津神}} were traditionally rebuilt using public funds, but six highly Ranked shrines, including this one, were rebuilt by the shrine’s construction officials after the Completion tier 4]] The shrine forest is composed of large trees that survived the [[:en:Typhoon Vera|Isewan Typhoon]], including [[:en:Chamaecyparis obtusa|Japanese cypress]], [[:en:Cryptomeria|cedar]], and [[:en:Pinus thunbergii|Japanese black pine]]. It also contains a mixture of evergreen broadleaf trees such as [[:en:Cleyera japonica|sakaki]], [[:en:Castanopsis|Japanese chinquapin]], {{ill|black oak|ja|クロバイ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/black oak|qq=}}, and [[:en:Eurya japonica|Japanese cleyera]], along with deciduous broadleaf trees.<ref name="sk0"/>
== Surroundings ==
The shrine entrance faces {{ill|Mie Prefectural Route 22 Ise-Nantō Line|ja|三重県道22号伊勢南島線|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mie Prefectural Route 22 Ise-Nantō Line|qq=}}.<ref name="gk"/><ref>Ise Bunka-sha (2008): p. 70</ref> Despite its location along the roadway, the precincts remain peaceful and serene.<ref name="ic"/> Visitors coming from {{ill|Yamada (Ise)|ja|山田 (伊勢市)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamada (Ise)|qq=}} pass under the overpass of the [[:en:Ise Expressway|Ise Expressway]] to reach the shrine.<ref name="ic"/>
* From [[:en:Iseshi Station|Ise-shi Station]] on the [[:en:Sangū Line|Sangū Line]] operated by [[:en:Central Japan Railway Company|JR Central]] or the [[:en:Yamada Line (Kintetsu)|Kintetsu Yamada Line]] operated by [[:en:Kintetsu Railway|Kintetsu]], take a [[:en:Mie Kotsu|Mie Kotsu]] bus and get off at the ''Shiraki'' bus stop. The shrine is located immediately nearby.<ref name="tm"/> (Approx. 7.5 km bus distance,<ref name="sk9"/> about 19 minutes.<ref name="tm"/>)
* By car, it takes about 11 minutes (approx. 7.5 km) from {{ill|Tamaki IC|ja|玉城インターチェンジ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamaki IC|qq=}} on the Ise Expressway, or about 27 minutes (approx. 7.3 km) from {{ill|Ise-nishi IC|ja|伊勢西インターチェンジ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise-nishi IC|qq=}}.
== References ==
{{Reflist|2}}
== 参考文献 == * [[伊勢文化舎]] 編『お伊勢さん125社めぐり』別冊『伊勢人』、伊勢文化舎、平成20th year 12月23日、151p. ISBN 978-4-900759-37-4 * 宇治山田市役所 編『宇治山田市史 上巻』宇治山田市役所、昭和4th year 1月20日、862p. * [[学研パブリッシング]]『伊勢神宮に行こう』Gakken Mook神社紀行セレクションvol.1、[[薗田稔]]監修、学研マーケティング、2013rd year 7月4日、82p. ISBN 978-4-05-610047-1 * 金子延夫『玉城町史 第1巻―南伊勢の歴史と伝承―』三重県郷土資料叢書第61集、三重県郷土史料刊行会、昭和58th year 8月10日、276p. * 式内社研究会 編『式内社調査報告 第六巻 東海道1』[[Kogakkan University]]出版部、平成2nd year 2月28日、690p. ISBN 4-87644-080-8 * [[福山敏男]]・[[稲垣栄三|稲垣榮三]]・村瀬美樹・胡麻鶴醇之『神宮―第六十回神宮式年遷宮―』[[小学館]]、昭和50th year 4月20日、246p. * 『日本人なら知っておきたい 伊勢神宮と125の社』タツミムック、[[辰巳出版]]、2013rd year 11月1日、127p. ISBN 978-4-7778-1197-7 == See Also == * {{ill|List of 125 Shrines of Ise|ja|神宮125社の一覧|lt=List of 125 Shrines of Ise|1=List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|3=伊勢国の式内社一覧|1=List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of 125 Shrines of Ise|13=User:Immanuelle/List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|qq=}} * [[宮本村 (三重県)]]
== External Links == {{ external media | align = | width = 230px | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image1 = [http://www.sengukan.jp/wp-content/themes/sengukan/images/shrine/area05/area05_01.jpg 園相神社] }} * [http://www.jingukaikan.jp/125mairi/m06/0608.html 園相神社（そないじんじゃ）] - 一般財団法人伊勢神宮崇敬会
{{神道 横}}
{{125 Shrines of Ise}}
(1467–1468), reconstruction efforts began to delay, and eventually, not only did reconstruction cease, but the location of the shrine grounds also became unknown.<ref name="sk9"/>
In [[:en:Kanbun (era)|Kanbun]] 3 (1663), the chief priest {{ill|Kawabe Masanaga|ja|河邊精長|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kawabe Masanaga|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Ōnakatomi Masanaga) conducted a search for the old shrine grounds. Referring to descriptions in the ''Kōtai Jingū Gishiki-chō'' such as "located in Numaki-go Sekiryo," the shrine was reconstructed in Sekiryo Village (present-day [[:en:Tamaki, Mie|Tamaki, Mie]] Sekiryo) on [[:en:September 22|September 22]] (Gregorian calendar: October 22, 1663), and the deity was transferred on [[:en:December 9|December 9]] (Gregorian calendar: January 7, 1664).<ref name="sk9"/>
However, doubts arose regarding this location, and on [[:en:December 23|December 23]] of [[:en:Genroku|Genroku]] 4 (Gregorian calendar: February 9, 1692), Ōnakatomi Nagaharu determined that Tsumura was the original site. The shrine was relocated to its current location on [[:en:March 26|March 26]] of Genroku 7 (Gregorian calendar: April 20, 1694).<ref name="sk9"/>
The shrine was rebuilt in March [[:en:1958|1958]] (Shōwa 33), and major renovations were carried out in April [[:en:1981|1981]] (Shōwa 56).<ref name="sk1"/>
== Rituals ==
During major festivals such as {{ill|Tsukinami-sai|ja|月次祭|1=Tsukinami-no-Matsuri|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukinami-sai|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukinami-no-Matsuri|qq=}}, [[:en:Kannamesai Festival|Kannamesai]], [[:en:Kinen-sai|Kinensai]], [[:en:Niiname-no-Matsuri|Niinamesai]], and temporary offerings, priests visit the shrine to conduct rituals at the shrine front.<ref name="sk0"/> Other ceremonies, such as the {{ill|Saitan-sai|ja|歳旦祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saitan-sai|qq=}}, are conducted remotely from the Gojōden of the Inner Shrine.<ref name="sk0"/>
Additionally, the role of {{ill|Hōri|lt=Hōri|lt=Hafuri (Shinto)|ja|祝部|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/hafuri (Shinto)|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 祝 (神職)}} is designated to carry out daily maintenance and shrine duties. The hafuri are appointed by the Jingū Administration Office.<ref name="sk0"/>
== Flora ==
[[File:Sonai-jinja 02.jpg|thumb|Sacred forest of Sonai Shrine and Mie Prefectural Route 22 Ise-Nantō Line]]
}}
{{translated page|ja|園相神社|version=77002198|comment=Imported full ja history}}
{{draft categories| [[Category: of the Inner Shrine’s transfer ceremony.<ref>Kaneko (1983): pp. 60–61</ref> Records of such a ceremony can be found in the ''Bunei 3 Naikū Sengu-ki''.<ref name="sk9">Shikinaisha Research Association (1990): p. 219</ref>
However, from around the [[:en:Ōnin|Ōnin era]]

'''{{ill|Sonai Shrine|ja|園相神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sonai Shrine|qq=}}''' (*Sonai Jinja*) is an auxiliary shrine (*sessha*) of {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jingū|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jingū|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} (the Inner Shrine of the [[Ise Grand Shrine]]), located in [[Ise]], [[Mie Prefecture]], Japan. It Ranks third among the auxiliary shrines of the Inner Shrine.<ref>Ujiyamada City Office (1929): pp. 9–11.</ref>
The shrine enshrines deities who serve as the guardians of the sacred gardens (*御園*, *misono*) of the Grand Shrine.<ref name="gk">Gakken Publishing (2013): p. 65.</ref><ref name="ic">Ise Bunka Publishing (2008): p. 71.</ref>
== Overview ==
Located at 723 Shiraki, Tsumura-chō, [[:en:Ise, Mie|Ise, Mie]], [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]].<ref name="tm"/> This shrine is said to have been established by [[:en:Yamatohime-no-mikoto|Yamatohime-no-mikoto]].<ref name="gk"/> Locally, it is affectionately referred to as "Shiraki-san" or "Shiroki-no-miya," and has garnered deep devotion.<ref name="ic"/>
Walking through a straight path lined with tall {{ill|cedar trees|ja|杉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/cedar trees|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スギ}}, one reaches the shrine buildings.<ref name="ic"/> At the entrance of the path stands a stone inscribed in [[:en:Kyōhō|Kyōhō]] (1724), prohibiting the killing of living beings.<ref name="sk1">Shikinaisha Research Association (1990): p. 221</ref> As of 1990 (Heisei 2), the shrine grounds covered 5,609 m², but 753.9 m² were transferred to Mie Prefecture for the expansion of {{ill|Mie Prefectural Route 22 Ise-Nantō Line|ja|三重県道22号伊勢南島線|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mie Prefectural Route 22 Ise-Nantō Line|qq=}}.<ref name="sk1"/> The shrine buildings are constructed in the [[:en:Shinmei-zukuri|Shinmei-zukuri]] style with board roofing, measuring approximately 9 shaku (≈2.7 m) in length, 7 shaku (≈2.1 m) in width, and 7.8 shaku (≈2.4 m) in height.<ref name="sk1"/> They are equipped with a sacred fence ([[:en:Tamagaki|tamagaki]]), a tamagaki gate, and a [[:en:Torii|torii]].<ref name="sk1"/>
In ancient times, there were two shrine buildings, each measuring 9 shaku (≈2.7 m) in length, 7.5 shaku (≈2.3 m) in width, and 4.6 shaku (≈1.4 m) in height.<ref name="jg">Fukuyama et al. (1975): p. 150</ref> The sacred object is a stone.<ref name="jg"/>
== Enshrined Deities ==
The enshrined deities are Sonaibihiko-no-Mikoto (曽奈比比古命) and Gozen-no-Kami (御前神).<ref name="gk"/><ref name="tm"/><ref name="ic"/> They are local deities of this region<ref name="gk"/> and protectors of the sacred gardens of Ise Grand Shrine.<ref name="ic"/> Sonaibihiko-no-Mikoto is the child of Ōminakami-no-Mikoto (大水上命) and is known as the deity of garden creation.<ref name="tm"/>
There was once a belief that the shrine could cure toothaches, and it was customary to offer 12 pairs of chopsticks made from bush clover as a token of gratitude upon recovery.<ref name="ic"/>
In ancient times, there was an independent shrine dedicated to Gozen-no-Kami, but after its reconstruction in [[:en:Bun'ei|Bunei]] 3 (1266), no further reconstructions took place. Eventually, the shrine was not rebuilt, and Gozen-no-Kami came to be enshrined together with Sonaibihiko-no-Mikoto in the main shrine.<ref>Shikinaisha Research Association (1990): pp. 219–220</ref>
== History ==
It is said that when [[:en:Yamatohime-no-mikoto|Yamatohime-no-mikoto]] traveled along the [[:en:Miyagawa|Miyagawa River (Mie)]], she dedicated sacred gardens to [[:en:Amaterasu|Amaterasu Ōmikami]] at this location, thus establishing the shrine.<ref name="ic"/><ref name="sk0">Shikinaisha Research Association (1990): p. 220</ref> Shrines enshrining {{ill|kunitsukami|ja|国津神|1=Kunitsukami|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kunitsukami|13=User:Immanuelle/Kunitsukami|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天津神・国津神}} were traditionally rebuilt using public funds, but six highly Ranked shrines, including this one, were rebuilt by the shrine’s construction officials after the Completion tier 4]] The shrine forest is composed of large trees that survived the [[:en:Typhoon Vera|Isewan Typhoon]], including [[:en:Chamaecyparis obtusa|Japanese cypress]], [[:en:Cryptomeria|cedar]], and [[:en:Pinus thunbergii|Japanese black pine]]. It also contains a mixture of evergreen broadleaf trees such as [[:en:Cleyera japonica|sakaki]], [[:en:Castanopsis|Japanese chinquapin]], {{ill|black oak|ja|クロバイ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/black oak|qq=}}, and [[:en:Eurya japonica|Japanese cleyera]], along with deciduous broadleaf trees.<ref name="sk0"/>
== Surroundings ==
The shrine entrance faces {{ill|Mie Prefectural Route 22 Ise-Nantō Line|ja|三重県道22号伊勢南島線|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mie Prefectural Route 22 Ise-Nantō Line|qq=}}.<ref name="gk"/><ref>Ise Bunka-sha (2008): p. 70</ref> Despite its location along the roadway, the precincts remain peaceful and serene.<ref name="ic"/> Visitors coming from {{ill|Yamada (Ise)|ja|山田 (伊勢市)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamada (Ise)|qq=}} pass under the overpass of the [[:en:Ise Expressway|Ise Expressway]] to reach the shrine.<ref name="ic"/>
* From [[:en:Iseshi Station|Ise-shi Station]] on the [[:en:Sangū Line|Sangū Line]] operated by [[:en:Central Japan Railway Company|JR Central]] or the [[:en:Yamada Line (Kintetsu)|Kintetsu Yamada Line]] operated by [[:en:Kintetsu Railway|Kintetsu]], take a [[:en:Mie Kotsu|Mie Kotsu]] bus and get off at the ''Shiraki'' bus stop. The shrine is located immediately nearby.<ref name="tm"/> (Approx. 7.5 km bus distance,<ref name="sk9"/> about 19 minutes.<ref name="tm"/>)
* By car, it takes about 11 minutes (approx. 7.5 km) from {{ill|Tamaki IC|ja|玉城インターチェンジ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamaki IC|qq=}} on the Ise Expressway, or about 27 minutes (approx. 7.3 km) from {{ill|Ise-nishi IC|ja|伊勢西インターチェンジ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise-nishi IC|qq=}}.

ISBN 978-4-7778-1197-7 == See Also == * {{ill|List of 125 Shrines of Ise|ja|神宮125社の一覧|lt=List of 125 Shrines of Ise|1=List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|3=伊勢国の式内社一覧|1=List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of 125 Shrines of Ise|13=User:Immanuelle/List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|qq=}} * [[宮本村 (三重県)]]
== External Links == {{ external media | align = | width = 230px | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image1 = [http://www.sengukan.jp/wp-content/themes/sengukan/images/shrine/area05/area05_01.jpg 園相神社] }} * [http://www.jingukaikan.jp/125mairi/m06/0608.html 園相神社（そないじんじゃ）] - 一般財団法人伊勢神宮崇敬会
{{神道 横}}
{{125 Shrines of Ise}}
(1467–1468), reconstruction efforts began to delay, and eventually, not only did reconstruction cease, but the location of the shrine grounds also became unknown.<ref name="sk9"/>
In [[:en:Kanbun (era)|Kanbun]] 3 (1663), the chief priest {{ill|Kawabe Masanaga|ja|河邊精長|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kawabe Masanaga|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Ōnakatomi Masanaga) conducted a search for the old shrine grounds. Referring to descriptions in the ''Kōtai Jingū Gishiki-chō'' such as "located in Numaki-go Sekiryo," the shrine was reconstructed in Sekiryo Village (present-day [[:en:Tamaki, Mie|Tamaki, Mie]] Sekiryo) on [[:en:September 22|September 22]] (Gregorian calendar: October 22, 1663), and the deity was transferred on [[:en:December 9|December 9]] (Gregorian calendar: January 7, 1664).<ref name="sk9"/>
However, doubts arose regarding this location, and on [[:en:December 23|December 23]] of [[:en:Genroku|Genroku]] 4 (Gregorian calendar: February 9, 1692), Ōnakatomi Nagaharu determined that Tsumura was the original site. The shrine was relocated to its current location on [[:en:March 26|March 26]] of Genroku 7 (Gregorian calendar: April 20, 1694).<ref name="sk9"/>
The shrine was rebuilt in March [[:en:1958|1958]] (Shōwa 33), and major renovations were carried out in April [[:en:1981|1981]] (Shōwa 56).<ref name="sk1"/>
== Rituals ==
During major festivals such as {{ill|Tsukinami-sai|ja|月次祭|1=Tsukinami-no-Matsuri|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukinami-sai|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukinami-no-Matsuri|qq=}}, [[:en:Kannamesai Festival|Kannamesai]], [[:en:Kinen-sai|Kinensai]], [[:en:Niiname-no-Matsuri|Niinamesai]], and temporary offerings, priests visit the shrine to conduct rituals at the shrine front.<ref name="sk0"/> Other ceremonies, such as the {{ill|Saitan-sai|ja|歳旦祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saitan-sai|qq=}}, are conducted remotely from the Gojōden of the Inner Shrine.<ref name="sk0"/>
Additionally, the role of {{ill|Hōri|lt=Hōri|lt=Hafuri (Shinto)|ja|祝部|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/hafuri (Shinto)|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 祝 (神職)}} is designated to carry out daily maintenance and shrine duties. The hafuri are appointed by the Jingū Administration Office.<ref name="sk0"/>
== Flora ==
[[File:Sonai-jinja 02.jpg|thumb|Sacred forest of Sonai Shrine and Mie Prefectural Route 22 Ise-Nantō Line]]
}}

revision.text:
{{Shrine | Name = {{ill|Sonai Shrine|ja|園相神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sonai Shrine|qq=}} | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = [[File:Sonai-jinja 01.jpg|280px]] | location = 723 Shiraki, Tsumura-cho, [[Ise]], [[Mie Prefecture]], Japan | coordinates = {{coord|34|27|25.1|N|136|39|19.7|E|region:JP|display=inline}} | Map = Japan Mie | deity = ** {{ill|Sonaibihiko-no-mikoto|ja|曽奈比比古命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sonaibihiko-no-mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} ** {{ill|Mimae-no-kami|ja|御前神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mimae-no-kami|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} | type = [[Shikinaisha]] (minor Rank), {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jingū|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jingū|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} auxiliary shrine (*sessha*) | Established = Unknown | Main Hall = [[Shinmei-zukuri]] | Other Names = Shiraki-san (白木さん) | festival = [[Kannamesai Festival|Kannamesai]] (Harvest Festival) | Rituals = }}
'''{{ill|Sonai Shrine|ja|園相神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sonai Shrine|qq=}}''' (*Sonai Jinja*) is an auxiliary shrine (*sessha*) of {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jingū|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jingū|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} (the Inner Shrine of the [[Ise Grand Shrine]]), located in [[Ise]], [[Mie Prefecture]], Japan. It Ranks third among the auxiliary shrines of the Inner Shrine.<ref>Ujiyamada City Office (1929): pp. 9–11.</ref>
The shrine enshrines deities who serve as the guardians of the sacred gardens (*御園*, *misono*) of the Grand Shrine.<ref name="gk">Gakken Publishing (2013): p. 65.</ref><ref name="ic">Ise Bunka Publishing (2008): p. 71.</ref>
== Overview ==
Located at 723 Shiraki, Tsumura-chō, [[:en:Ise, Mie|Ise, Mie]], [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]].<ref name="tm"/> This shrine is said to have been established by [[:en:Yamatohime-no-mikoto|Yamatohime-no-mikoto]].<ref name="gk"/> Locally, it is affectionately referred to as "Shiraki-san" or "Shiroki-no-miya," and has garnered deep devotion.<ref name="ic"/>
Walking through a straight path lined with tall {{ill|cedar trees|ja|杉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/cedar trees|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スギ}}, one reaches the shrine buildings.<ref name="ic"/> At the entrance of the path stands a stone inscribed in [[:en:Kyōhō|Kyōhō]] (1724), prohibiting the killing of living beings.<ref name="sk1">Shikinaisha Research Association (1990): p. 221</ref> As of 1990 (Heisei 2), the shrine grounds covered 5,609 m², but 753.9 m² were transferred to Mie Prefecture for the expansion of {{ill|Mie Prefectural Route 22 Ise-Nantō Line|ja|三重県道22号伊勢南島線|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mie Prefectural Route 22 Ise-Nantō Line|qq=}}.<ref name="sk1"/> The shrine buildings are constructed in the [[:en:Shinmei-zukuri|Shinmei-zukuri]] style with board roofing, measuring approximately 9 shaku (≈2.7 m) in length, 7 shaku (≈2.1 m) in width, and 7.8 shaku (≈2.4 m) in height.<ref name="sk1"/> They are equipped with a sacred fence ([[:en:Tamagaki|tamagaki]]), a tamagaki gate, and a [[:en:Torii|torii]].<ref name="sk1"/>
In ancient times, there were two shrine buildings, each measuring 9 shaku (≈2.7 m) in length, 7.5 shaku (≈2.3 m) in width, and 4.6 shaku (≈1.4 m) in height.<ref name="jg">Fukuyama et al. (1975): p. 150</ref> The sacred object is a stone.<ref name="jg"/>
== Enshrined Deities ==
The enshrined deities are Sonaibihiko-no-Mikoto (曽奈比比古命) and Gozen-no-Kami (御前神).<ref name="gk"/><ref name="tm"/><ref name="ic"/> They are local deities of this region<ref name="gk"/> and protectors of the sacred gardens of Ise Grand Shrine.<ref name="ic"/> Sonaibihiko-no-Mikoto is the child of Ōminakami-no-Mikoto (大水上命) and is known as the deity of garden creation.<ref name="tm"/>
There was once a belief that the shrine could cure toothaches, and it was customary to offer 12 pairs of chopsticks made from bush clover as a token of gratitude upon recovery.<ref name="ic"/>
In ancient times, there was an independent shrine dedicated to Gozen-no-Kami, but after its reconstruction in [[:en:Bun'ei|Bunei]] 3 (1266), no further reconstructions took place. Eventually, the shrine was not rebuilt, and Gozen-no-Kami came to be enshrined together with Sonaibihiko-no-Mikoto in the main shrine.<ref>Shikinaisha Research Association (1990): pp. 219–220</ref>
== History ==
It is said that when [[:en:Yamatohime-no-mikoto|Yamatohime-no-mikoto]] traveled along the [[:en:Miyagawa|Miyagawa River (Mie)]], she dedicated sacred gardens to [[:en:Amaterasu|Amaterasu Ōmikami]] at this location, thus establishing the shrine.<ref name="ic"/><ref name="sk0">Shikinaisha Research Association (1990): p. 220</ref> Shrines enshrining {{ill|kunitsukami|ja|国津神|1=Kunitsukami|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kunitsukami|13=User:Immanuelle/Kunitsukami|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天津神・国津神}} were traditionally rebuilt using public funds, but six highly Ranked shrines, including this one, were rebuilt by the shrine’s construction officials after the Completion tier 4]] The shrine forest is composed of large trees that survived the [[:en:Typhoon Vera|Isewan Typhoon]], including [[:en:Chamaecyparis obtusa|Japanese cypress]], [[:en:Cryptomeria|cedar]], and [[:en:Pinus thunbergii|Japanese black pine]]. It also contains a mixture of evergreen broadleaf trees such as [[:en:Cleyera japonica|sakaki]], [[:en:Castanopsis|Japanese chinquapin]], {{ill|black oak|ja|クロバイ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/black oak|qq=}}, and [[:en:Eurya japonica|Japanese cleyera]], along with deciduous broadleaf trees.<ref name="sk0"/>
== Surroundings ==
The shrine entrance faces {{ill|Mie Prefectural Route 22 Ise-Nantō Line|ja|三重県道22号伊勢南島線|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mie Prefectural Route 22 Ise-Nantō Line|qq=}}.<ref name="gk"/><ref>Ise Bunka-sha (2008): p. 70</ref> Despite its location along the roadway, the precincts remain peaceful and serene.<ref name="ic"/> Visitors coming from {{ill|Yamada (Ise)|ja|山田 (伊勢市)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamada (Ise)|qq=}} pass under the overpass of the [[:en:Ise Expressway|Ise Expressway]] to reach the shrine.<ref name="ic"/>
* From [[:en:Iseshi Station|Ise-shi Station]] on the [[:en:Sangū Line|Sangū Line]] operated by [[:en:Central Japan Railway Company|JR Central]] or the [[:en:Yamada Line (Kintetsu)|Kintetsu Yamada Line]] operated by [[:en:Kintetsu Railway|Kintetsu]], take a [[:en:Mie Kotsu|Mie Kotsu]] bus and get off at the ''Shiraki'' bus stop. The shrine is located immediately nearby.<ref name="tm"/> (Approx. 7.5 km bus distance,<ref name="sk9"/> about 19 minutes.<ref name="tm"/>)
* By car, it takes about 11 minutes (approx. 7.5 km) from {{ill|Tamaki IC|ja|玉城インターチェンジ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamaki IC|qq=}} on the Ise Expressway, or about 27 minutes (approx. 7.3 km) from {{ill|Ise-nishi IC|ja|伊勢西インターチェンジ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise-nishi IC|qq=}}.
== References ==
{{Reflist|2}}

ISBN 978-4-900759-37-4 * 宇治山田市役所 編『宇治山田市史 上巻』宇治山田市役所、昭和4th year 1月20日、862p. * [[:en:学研パブリッシング|学研パブリッシング]]『伊勢神宮に行こう』Gakken Mook神社紀行セレクションvol.1、[[薗田稔]]監修、学研マーケティング、2013rd year 7月4日、82p. ISBN 978-4-05-610047-1 * 金子延夫『玉城町史 第1巻―南伊勢の歴史と伝承―』三重県郷土資料叢書第61集、三重県郷土史料刊行会、昭和58th year 8月10日、276p. * 式内社研究会 編『式内社調査報告 第六巻 東海道1』[[Kogakkan University]]出版部、平成2nd year 2月28日、690p. ISBN 4-87644-080-8 * [[福山敏男]]・[[稲垣栄三|稲垣榮三]]・村瀬美樹・胡麻鶴醇之『神宮―第六十回神宮式年遷宮―』[[:en:小学館|小学館]]、昭和50th year 4月20日、246p. * 『日本人なら知っておきたい 伊勢神宮と125の社』タツミムック、[[辰巳出版]]、2013rd year 11月1日、127p. ISBN 978-4-7778-1197-7 == See Also == * {{ill|List of 125 Shrines of Ise|ja|神宮125社の一覧|lt=List of 125 Shrines of Ise|1=List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|3=伊勢国の式内社一覧|1=List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of 125 Shrines of Ise|13=User:Immanuelle/List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|qq=}} * [[宮本村 (三重県)]]

In [[:en:Kanbun (era)|Kanbun]] 3 (1663), the chief priest {{ill|Kawabe Masanaga|ja|河邊精長|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kawabe Masanaga|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Ōnakatomi Masanaga) conducted a search for the old shrine grounds. However, doubts arose regarding this location, and on [[:en:December 23|December 23]] of [[:en:Genroku|Genroku]] 4 (Gregorian calendar: February 9, 1692), Ōnakatomi Nagaharu determined that Tsumura was the original site. The hafuri are appointed by the Jingū Administration Office.<ref name="sk0"/>

The shrine enshrines deities who serve as the guardians of the sacred gardens (*御園*, *misono*) of the Grand Shrine.<ref name="gk">Gakken Publishing (2013): p. 65.</ref><ref name="ic">Ise Bunka Publishing (2008): p. 71.</ref>

In ancient times, there were two shrine buildings, each measuring 9 shaku (≈2.7 m) in length, 7.5 shaku (≈2.3 m) in width, and 4.6 shaku (≈1.4 m) in height.<ref name="jg">Fukuyama et al. (1975): p. 150</ref> The sacred object is a stone.<ref name="jg"/>
